> I'm thinking about the changing [Port](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/api/core/v1/types.go#L2191) type of GRPCAction struct to intstr.IntOrString.

i'm not sure why that was mentioned in this pull request, but that's not a compatible change to make, and would break existing clients, so we can't do that

floats are discouraged in API types (https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#primitive-types) and often have issues perfectly round-tripping

is there a specific type you are encountering issues with you can link to?

That was an explicit design choice when adding GRPC actions. From https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-node/2727-grpc-probe/README.md#design-details:

> Note that GRPCAction.Port is an int32, which is inconsistent with the other existing probe definitions. This is on purpose -- we want to move users away from using the (portNum, portName) union type.

Even if it wasn't intentional and we *did* want to change it, [all API clients built since 1.23.0](https://github.com/kubernetes/api/blob/v0.23.0/core/v1/types.go#L2159-L2161) would break if the server ever started returning string values in `port` field of a grpc action, so that wouldn't be a change we could make.
I misread, this is tightening validation, so anything this allows to be persisted, 1.26 will be equally happy with.
Otherwise, the PR lgtm
/approve

Reading further, it looks like git only tracks the executable bit, and group/other permissions are solely governed by the user's umask when checking out.

That means that generating this with 0600 will likely produce fixtures with 0600 permissions, but that once they are committed and checked out, the file permissions will likely still be 0644. But since they are test fixture files being committed to source, that doesn't really matter anyway.

Generating fixtures on the fly for unit tests was expensive on underpowered CI systems and led to problems. The fixtures this leads to are things like https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/tree/master/cmd/kube-apiserver/app/testing/testdata which are never used in the context of a server which can be used to create real executing workloads, only unit/integration tests which are immediately torn down and deleted.

/lgtm
/approve

> Not sure if there is precedent to configuring the defaulting logic instead, like this issue is proposing.

There's ~no precedent I'm aware of for configurable API-level defaulting. API-level defaults are expected to be deterministic and identical across API servers and versions that are aware of the field being defaulted.

Making it configurable introduces *many* problematic edge cases (what if different components are configured differently? different instances of components are configured differently? what impact does changing the configuration have on existing data?) clusterIP is the closest thing I can think of to a configurable "default" (it got set on write if blank in the Service REST handler, not actually in API defaulting), and it has been extremely problematic over the years.

> He wants to limit the CPU usage of the set of all pods in the namespace to 8, which should be possible via cgroup.

cgroups are local to the node running the pod, but pods in a namespace are not all on the same node. That means real time CPU usage would have to be reported back into the API, observed by other nodes, and cgroup rules updated in response, at high frequency, for this to work. You need some indicator on each pod how the CPU limits should be set to inform the node how to set its cgroups.

yes, as well as look for places where we are constructing `authorization.ResourceAttributes` with blank version fields like https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/controller/certificates/approver/sarapprove.go#L64-L73 and change them to `*`

we trimmed the main CEL expression for two reasons:
1. because it was going to be used as the default error message in failure cases, and we wanted to require a separate human-readable message if the CEL expression contained newlines after trimming
2. making sure it was non-empty after trimming space was a cheap check before getting into CEL parsing

	// Version is the API Version of the Resource.  "*" means all.

+// Authz provides a CEL function library extension for performing authorization checks.

+//

+//	authorizer.group('') // returns a GroupCheck for the core API group

+//

+//

+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'pods' resources in the core group.
+//	authorizer.group('example.com').resource('widgets') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'widgets' custom resources in the 'example.com' group.
+//
+// subresource
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular subresource of an API resource.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.subresource(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').subresource('status') // returns a ResourceCheck the 'status' subresource of 'pods'
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').subresource('scale') // returns a ResourceCheck the 'scale' subresource of 'deployments'
+//	authorizer.group('example.com').resource('widgets').subresource('scale') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'scale' subresource of the 'widgets' custom resource

+//

+}
+
+func NewResourceAuthorizerVal(userInfo user.Info, authorizer authorizer.Authorizer, requestResource Resource) ref.Val {
+	a := authorizerVal{receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(AuthorizerType), userInfo: userInfo, authAuthorizer: authorizer}
+	resource := requestResource.GetResource()
+	g := a.groupCheck(resource.Group)
+	r := g.resourceCheck(resource.Resource)
+	r.subresource = requestResource.GetSubresource()
+	r.namespace = requestResource.GetNamespace()
+	r.name = requestResource.GetName()
+	return r
+}
+
+type authorizerVal struct {
+	receiverOnlyObjectVal
+	userInfo       user.Info
+	authAuthorizer authorizer.Authorizer
+}
+
+func (a authorizerVal) pathCheck(path string) pathCheckVal {
+	return pathCheckVal{receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(PathCheckType), authorizer: a, path: path}
+}
+
+func (a authorizerVal) groupCheck(group string) groupCheckVal {
+	return groupCheckVal{receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(GroupCheckType), authorizer: a, group: group}
+}
+
+type pathCheckVal struct {
+	receiverOnlyObjectVal
+	authorizer authorizerVal
+	path       string
+}
+
+func (a pathCheckVal) Authorize(ctx context.Context, verb string) ref.Val {
+	attr := &authorizer.AttributesRecord{
+		Path: a.path,
+		Verb: verb,
+		User: a.authorizer.userInfo,
+	}
+
+	decision, reason, err := a.authorizer.authAuthorizer.Authorize(ctx, attr)
+	if err != nil {
+		return types.NewErr("error in authorization check: %v", err)
+	}
+	return newDecision(decision, reason)
+}
+
+type groupCheckVal struct {
+	receiverOnlyObjectVal
+	authorizer authorizerVal
+	group      string
+}
+
+func (g groupCheckVal) resourceCheck(resource string) resourceCheckVal {
+	return resourceCheckVal{receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(ResourceCheckType), groupCheck: g, resource: resource}
+}
+
+type resourceCheckVal struct {
+	receiverOnlyObjectVal
+	groupCheck  groupCheckVal
+	resource    string
+	subresource string
+	namespace   string
+	name        string
+}
+
+func (a resourceCheckVal) Authorize(ctx context.Context, verb string) ref.Val {
+	attr := &authorizer.AttributesRecord{
+		ResourceRequest: true,
+		APIGroup:        a.groupCheck.group,
+		APIVersion:      "*",

/remove-kind bug
/kind support

In place upgrades of kubelet with running pods are not supported (see https://kubernetes.io/releases/version-skew-policy/#kubelet-1). Kubelet does not guarantee on-disk structures associated with running pods are compatible across versions. Before minor version upgrades, drain API controlled pods and remove static pod manifests. 

The PR you referenced made kubelet less vulnerable to disruptions by API server upgrades, but did not change support for in place upgrades with running containers. 

/close

/lgtm

/sig api-machinery

For multi-protocol services we haven't ever synced (where the `Status.LoadBalancer.Ingress` list is empty), skipping services we can't satisfy makes sense.

For multi-protocol services we've already synced, it's unclear to me what we should do.
* Ignoring updates to those services can leave previously exposed ports exposed via load-balancer in ways that that would surprise users or cause problems.
* Unconditionally cleaning up the load balancer could cause outages.

Should we continue syncing previously-synced services ignoring ports that don't match the configured protocol of the load balancer and add a condition for visibility?

I'm not sure we should add this dependency.

First, do we need to? It looks like the checkpoints are written in a format we can scan and sort by time... is there a reason not to make the cleanup function glob, sort by time, and delete the oldest N checkpoints?

Second, this adds a dependency that has a decent number of transitive dependencies, which usually only increase over time ... https://github.com/checkpoint-restore/checkpointctl/blob/main/go.mod

Third, this makes us depend on a persisted file controlled by an external library, which the compatibility guarantees around are unclear.

comment_text:
> So how do we deal when v1alpha1 and v1alpha2 have divergent data structures ?

You must provide a conversion webhook that can round-trip between those structures losslessly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/extend-kubernetes/custom-resources/custom-resource-definition-versioning/ for details

> Since custom resource objects need the ability to be served at both versions, that means they will sometimes be served in a different version than the one stored. To make this possible, the custom resource objects must sometimes be converted between the version they are stored at and the version they are served at. If the conversion involves schema changes and requires custom logic, a conversion webhook should be used. If there are no schema changes, the default `None` conversion strategy may be used and only the `apiVersion` field will be modified when serving different versions.

is that related to this change?

> it's unclear how we can change the types to be able to change behavior per request

I don't think we would ever modify existing type serialization of a field that way... the expectation is that every serialized Pod object can be successfully decoded by every client ever linked to Pod API types.

A parallel field is the only way I could see this working, and I agree with @lavalamp that that's a *lot* of work, has weird / problematic edge cases involving client incompatibility and data loss, and it's unclear that effort / risk is justified here.

comment_text:
let's drop `denied` ... `allowed=false,denied=false` is really only meaningful for something evaluating a chain of authorizers

we don't people to check `!denied` thinking that necessarily means `allowed=true`

+
+package library
+
+import (
+	"context"
+	"fmt"
+	"reflect"
+
+	"github.com/google/cel-go/cel"
+	"github.com/google/cel-go/common/types"
+	"github.com/google/cel-go/common/types/ref"
+
+	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime/schema"
+	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/authentication/serviceaccount"
+	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/authentication/user"
+	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/authorization/authorizer"
+)
+
+// Authz provides a CEL function library extension for performing authorization checks.
+//
+// path
+//
+// Returns a PathCheck configured to check authorization for a non-resource request
+// path (e.g. /healthz).
+// Note that authorization checks are only supported for CEL expression fields in the API
+// where an 'authorizer' variable is provided to the CEL expression. Check the
+// documentation of API fields where CEL expressions are used to learn if the 'authorizer'
+// variable is provided.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.path(<string>) <PathCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz') // returns a PathCheck for the '/healthz' API path
+//
+// group
+//
+// Returns a GroupCheck configured to check authorization for the API resources for
+// a particular API group.
+// Note that authorization checks are only supported for CEL expression fields in the API
+// where an 'authorizer' variable is provided to the CEL expression. Check the
+// documentation of API fields where CEL expressions are used to learn if the 'authorizer'
+// variable is provided.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.group(<string>) <GroupCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps') // returns a GroupCheck for the 'apps' API group
+//	authorizer.group('') // returns a GroupCheck for the core API group
+//	authorizer.group('example.com') // returns a GroupCheck for the custom resources in the 'example.com' API group
+//
+// serviceAccount
+//
+// Returns a Authorizer configured to check authorization for the provided service account namespace and name.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.serviceAccount(<string>, <string>) <Authorizer>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.serviceAccount('default', 'myserviceaccount') // returns a Authorizer for the service account with namespace 'default' and name 'myserviceaccount'
+//
+// resource
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular API resource.
+//
+//	<GroupCheck>.resource(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'deployments' resources in the 'apps' group.
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'pods' resources in the core group.
+//	authorizer.group('example.com').resource('widgets') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'widgets' custom resources in the 'example.com' group.
+//
+// subresource
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular subresource of an API resource.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.subresource(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').subresource('status') // returns a ResourceCheck the 'status' subresource of 'pods'
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').subresource('scale') // returns a ResourceCheck the 'scale' subresource of 'deployments'
+//	authorizer.group('example.com').resource('widgets').subresource('scale') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'scale' subresource of the 'widgets' custom resource
+//
+// namespace
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular namespace.
+// For cluster scoped resources, namespace() does not need to be called; namespace defaults
+// to "", which is the correct namespace value to use to check cluster scoped resources.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.namespace(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').namespace('test') // returns a ResourceCheck for 'deployments' in the 'test' namespace
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default') // returns a ResourceCheck for 'pods' in the 'default' namespace
+//
+// name
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular resource name.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.path(<name>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').namespace('test').name('backend') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'backend' 'deployments' resource in the 'test' namespace
+//
+// check
+//
+// For PathCheck, checks if the principal (user or service account) that sent the request is authorized for the HTTP request verb of the path.
+// For ResourceCheck, checks if the principal (user or service account) that sent the request is authorized for the API verb and the configured authorization checks of the ResourceCheck.
+//
+//	<PathCheck>.check(<check>) <Decision>
+//	<ResourceCheck>.check(<check>) <Decision>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default').check('create') // Checks if the principal (user or service account) is authorized create pods in the 'default' namespace.
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get') // Checks if the principal (user or service account) is authorized to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.
+//
+// allowed
+//
+// Returns true if the authorizer's decision for the check is "allow".  Note that if the authorizer's decision is
+// "no opinion", that both the 'allowed' and 'denied' functions will return false.
+//
+//	<Decision>.allowed() <bool>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default').check('create').allowed() // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is allowed create pods in the 'default' namespace.
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get').allowed()  // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is allowed to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.
+//
+// denied
+//
+// Returns true if the authorizer's decision for the check is "deny".  Note that if the authorizer's decision is
+// "no opinion", that both the 'allowed' and 'denied' functions will return false.
+//
+//	<Decision>.denied() <bool>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default').check('create').denied() // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is denied authorization to create pods in the 'default' namespace.
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get').denied()  // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is denied authorization to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.
+//
+// reason
+//
+// Returns a string reason for the authorization decision
+//
+//	<Decision>.reason() <string>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('GET').reason()
+func Authz() cel.EnvOption {
+	return cel.Lib(authzLib)
+}
+
+var authzLib = &authz{}
+
+type authz struct{}
+
+var authzLibraryDecls = map[string][]cel.FunctionOpt{
+	"path": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_path", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType}, PathCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(authorizerPath))},
+	"group": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_group", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType}, GroupCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(authorizerGroup))},
+	"serviceAccount": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_serviceaccount", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType, cel.StringType}, AuthorizerType,
+			cel.FunctionBinding(authorizerServiceAccount))},
+	"resource": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("groupcheck_resource", []*cel.Type{GroupCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(groupCheckResource))},
+	"subresource": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_subresource", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckSubresource))},
+	"namespace": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_namespace", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckNamespace))},
+	"name": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_name", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckName))},
+	"check": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("pathcheck_check", []*cel.Type{PathCheckType, cel.StringType}, DecisionType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(pathCheckCheck)),
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_check", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, DecisionType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckCheck))},
+	"allowed": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("decision_allowed", []*cel.Type{DecisionType}, cel.BoolType,
+			cel.UnaryBinding(decisionAllowed))},
+	"denied": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("decision_denied", []*cel.Type{DecisionType}, cel.BoolType,
+			cel.UnaryBinding(decisionDenied))},

comment_text:
I worry people will do this, which is really expensive:
```
expression: authorizer.path('/healthz').check('GET').allowed()
messageExpression: authorizer.path('/healthz').check('GET').reason()
```

maybe make authorizer not available to `messageExpression`, so the only way to get `reason()` there will be expression composition, and for now they'd just put a placeholder "you were not authorized" message

+}
+
+var authzLib = &authz{}
+
+type authz struct{}
+
+var authzLibraryDecls = map[string][]cel.FunctionOpt{
+	"path": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_path", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType}, PathCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(authorizerPath))},
+	"group": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_group", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType}, GroupCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(authorizerGroup))},
+	"serviceAccount": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_serviceaccount", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType, cel.StringType}, AuthorizerType,
+			cel.FunctionBinding(authorizerServiceAccount))},
+	"resource": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("groupcheck_resource", []*cel.Type{GroupCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(groupCheckResource))},
+	"subresource": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_subresource", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckSubresource))},
+	"namespace": {

+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckNamespace))},
+	"name": {

+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckName))},
+	"check": {

+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_check", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, DecisionType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckCheck))},
+	"allowed": {

+			cel.UnaryBinding(decisionReason))},

+	options := make([]cel.EnvOption, 0, len(authzLibraryDecls))

+	return options

+// Authz provides a CEL function library extension for performing authorization checks.
+//
+// path
+//
+// Returns a PathCheck configured to check authorization for a non-resource request
+// path (e.g. /healthz).
+// Note that authorization checks are only supported for CEL expression fields in the API
+// where an 'authorizer' variable is provided to the CEL expression. Check the
+// documentation of API fields where CEL expressions are used to learn if the 'authorizer'
+// variable is provided.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.path(<string>) <PathCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz') // returns a PathCheck for the '/healthz' API path
+//
+// group
+//
+// Returns a GroupCheck configured to check authorization for the API resources for
+// a particular API group.
+// Note that authorization checks are only supported for CEL expression fields in the API
+// where an 'authorizer' variable is provided to the CEL expression. Check the
+// documentation of API fields where CEL expressions are used to learn if the 'authorizer'
+// variable is provided.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.group(<string>) <GroupCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps') // returns a GroupCheck for the 'apps' API group
+//	authorizer.group('') // returns a GroupCheck for the core API group
+//	authorizer.group('example.com') // returns a GroupCheck for the custom resources in the 'example.com' API group
+//
+// serviceAccount
+//
+// Returns a Authorizer configured to check authorization for the provided service account namespace and name.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.serviceAccount(<string>, <string>) <Authorizer>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.serviceAccount('default', 'myserviceaccount') // returns a Authorizer for the service account with namespace 'default' and name 'myserviceaccount'
+//
+// resource
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular API resource.
+//
+//	<GroupCheck>.resource(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'deployments' resources in the 'apps' group.
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'pods' resources in the core group.
+//	authorizer.group('example.com').resource('widgets') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'widgets' custom resources in the 'example.com' group.
+//
+// subresource
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular subresource of an API resource.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.subresource(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').subresource('status') // returns a ResourceCheck the 'status' subresource of 'pods'
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').subresource('scale') // returns a ResourceCheck the 'scale' subresource of 'deployments'
+//	authorizer.group('example.com').resource('widgets').subresource('scale') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'scale' subresource of the 'widgets' custom resource
+//
+// namespace
+//

+// For cluster scoped resources, namespace() does not need to be called; namespace defaults
+// to "", which is the correct namespace value to use to check cluster scoped resources.

+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default') // returns a ResourceCheck for 'pods' in the 'default' namespace
+//
+// name
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular resource name.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.path(<name>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:

+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').namespace('test').name('backend') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'backend' 'deployments' resource in the 'test' namespace

+//

+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get') // Checks if the principal (user or service account) is authorized to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.
+//
+// allowed
+//
+// Returns true if the authorizer's decision for the check is "allow".  Note that if the authorizer's decision is

+//

+//
+// Examples:

+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default').check('create').allowed() // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is allowed create pods in the 'default' namespace.
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get').allowed()  // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is allowed to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.
+//
+// denied
+//
+// Returns true if the authorizer's decision for the check is "deny".  Note that if the authorizer's decision is

+//
+//	<Decision>.denied() <bool>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default').check('create').denied() // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is denied authorization to create pods in the 'default' namespace.
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get').denied()  // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is denied authorization to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.

+// Authz provides a CEL function library extension for performing authorization checks.
+//
+// path
+//
+// Returns a PathCheck configured to check authorization for a non-resource request
+// path (e.g. /healthz).
+// Note that authorization checks are only supported for CEL expression fields in the API
+// where an 'authorizer' variable is provided to the CEL expression. Check the
+// documentation of API fields where CEL expressions are used to learn if the 'authorizer'
+// variable is provided.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.path(<string>) <PathCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz') // returns a PathCheck for the '/healthz' API path
+//
+// group
+//
+// Returns a GroupCheck configured to check authorization for the API resources for
+// a particular API group.
+// Note that authorization checks are only supported for CEL expression fields in the API
+// where an 'authorizer' variable is provided to the CEL expression. Check the
+// documentation of API fields where CEL expressions are used to learn if the 'authorizer'
+// variable is provided.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.group(<string>) <GroupCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps') // returns a GroupCheck for the 'apps' API group
+//	authorizer.group('') // returns a GroupCheck for the core API group
+//	authorizer.group('example.com') // returns a GroupCheck for the custom resources in the 'example.com' API group
+//
+// serviceAccount
+//
+// Returns a Authorizer configured to check authorization for the provided service account namespace and name.
+//
+//	<Authorizer>.serviceAccount(<string>, <string>) <Authorizer>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.serviceAccount('default', 'myserviceaccount') // returns a Authorizer for the service account with namespace 'default' and name 'myserviceaccount'
+//
+// resource
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular API resource.
+//
+//	<GroupCheck>.resource(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'deployments' resources in the 'apps' group.
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'pods' resources in the core group.
+//	authorizer.group('example.com').resource('widgets') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'widgets' custom resources in the 'example.com' group.
+//
+// subresource
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular subresource of an API resource.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.subresource(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').subresource('status') // returns a ResourceCheck the 'status' subresource of 'pods'
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').subresource('scale') // returns a ResourceCheck the 'scale' subresource of 'deployments'
+//	authorizer.group('example.com').resource('widgets').subresource('scale') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'scale' subresource of the 'widgets' custom resource
+//
+// namespace
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular namespace.
+// For cluster scoped resources, namespace() does not need to be called; namespace defaults
+// to "", which is the correct namespace value to use to check cluster scoped resources.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.namespace(<string>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').namespace('test') // returns a ResourceCheck for 'deployments' in the 'test' namespace
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default') // returns a ResourceCheck for 'pods' in the 'default' namespace
+//
+// name
+//
+// Returns a ResourceCheck configured to check authorization for a particular resource name.
+//
+//	<ResourceCheck>.path(<name>) <ResourceCheck>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('apps').resource('deployments').namespace('test').name('backend') // returns a ResourceCheck for the 'backend' 'deployments' resource in the 'test' namespace
+//
+// check
+//
+// For PathCheck, checks if the principal (user or service account) that sent the request is authorized for the HTTP request verb of the path.
+// For ResourceCheck, checks if the principal (user or service account) that sent the request is authorized for the API verb and the configured authorization checks of the ResourceCheck.
+//
+//	<PathCheck>.check(<check>) <Decision>
+//	<ResourceCheck>.check(<check>) <Decision>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default').check('create') // Checks if the principal (user or service account) is authorized create pods in the 'default' namespace.
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get') // Checks if the principal (user or service account) is authorized to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.
+//
+// allowed
+//
+// Returns true if the authorizer's decision for the check is "allow".  Note that if the authorizer's decision is
+// "no opinion", that both the 'allowed' and 'denied' functions will return false.
+//
+//	<Decision>.allowed() <bool>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default').check('create').allowed() // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is allowed create pods in the 'default' namespace.
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get').allowed()  // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is allowed to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.
+//
+// denied
+//
+// Returns true if the authorizer's decision for the check is "deny".  Note that if the authorizer's decision is
+// "no opinion", that both the 'allowed' and 'denied' functions will return false.
+//
+//	<Decision>.denied() <bool>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.group('').resource('pods').namespace('default').check('create').denied() // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is denied authorization to create pods in the 'default' namespace.
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('get').denied()  // Returns true if the principal (user or service account) is denied authorization to make HTTP GET requests to the /healthz API path.
+//
+// reason
+//
+// Returns a string reason for the authorization decision
+//
+//	<Decision>.reason() <string>
+//
+// Examples:
+//
+//	authorizer.path('/healthz').check('GET').reason()
+func Authz() cel.EnvOption {
+	return cel.Lib(authzLib)
+}
+
+var authzLib = &authz{}
+
+type authz struct{}
+
+var authzLibraryDecls = map[string][]cel.FunctionOpt{
+	"path": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_path", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType}, PathCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(authorizerPath))},
+	"group": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_group", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType}, GroupCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(authorizerGroup))},
+	"serviceAccount": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("authorizer_serviceaccount", []*cel.Type{AuthorizerType, cel.StringType, cel.StringType}, AuthorizerType,
+			cel.FunctionBinding(authorizerServiceAccount))},
+	"resource": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("groupcheck_resource", []*cel.Type{GroupCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(groupCheckResource))},
+	"subresource": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_subresource", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckSubresource))},
+	"namespace": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_namespace", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckNamespace))},
+	"name": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_name", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, ResourceCheckType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckName))},
+	"check": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("pathcheck_check", []*cel.Type{PathCheckType, cel.StringType}, DecisionType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(pathCheckCheck)),
+		cel.MemberOverload("resourcecheck_check", []*cel.Type{ResourceCheckType, cel.StringType}, DecisionType,
+			cel.BinaryBinding(resourceCheckCheck))},
+	"allowed": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("decision_allowed", []*cel.Type{DecisionType}, cel.BoolType,
+			cel.UnaryBinding(decisionAllowed))},
+	"denied": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("decision_denied", []*cel.Type{DecisionType}, cel.BoolType,
+			cel.UnaryBinding(decisionDenied))},
+	"reason": {
+		cel.MemberOverload("decision_reason", []*cel.Type{DecisionType}, cel.StringType,
+			cel.UnaryBinding(decisionReason))},
+}
+
+func (*authz) CompileOptions() []cel.EnvOption {
+	options := make([]cel.EnvOption, 0, len(authzLibraryDecls))
+	for name, overloads := range authzLibraryDecls {
+		options = append(options, cel.Function(name, overloads...))
+	}
+	return options
+}
+
+func (*authz) ProgramOptions() []cel.ProgramOption {
+	return []cel.ProgramOption{}
+}
+
+func authorizerPath(arg1, arg2 ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	authz, ok := arg1.(authorizerVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	path, ok := arg2.Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	return authz.pathCheck(path)
+}
+
+func authorizerGroup(arg1, arg2 ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	authz, ok := arg1.(authorizerVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	group, ok := arg2.Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	return authz.groupCheck(group)
+}
+
+func authorizerServiceAccount(args ...ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	argn := len(args)
+	if argn != 3 {
+		return types.NoSuchOverloadErr()
+	}
+
+	authz, ok := args[0].(authorizerVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(args[0])
+	}
+
+	namespace, ok := args[1].Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(args[1])
+	}
+
+	name, ok := args[2].Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(args[2])
+	}
+
+	return authz.serviceAccount(namespace, name)
+}
+
+func groupCheckResource(arg1, arg2 ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	groupCheck, ok := arg1.(groupCheckVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	resource, ok := arg2.Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	return groupCheck.resourceCheck(resource)
+}
+
+func resourceCheckSubresource(arg1, arg2 ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	resourceCheck, ok := arg1.(resourceCheckVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	subresource, ok := arg2.Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	result := resourceCheck
+	result.subresource = subresource
+	return result
+}
+
+func resourceCheckNamespace(arg1, arg2 ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	resourceCheck, ok := arg1.(resourceCheckVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	namespace, ok := arg2.Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	result := resourceCheck
+	result.namespace = namespace
+	return result
+}
+
+func resourceCheckName(arg1, arg2 ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	resourceCheck, ok := arg1.(resourceCheckVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	name, ok := arg2.Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	result := resourceCheck
+	result.name = name
+	return result
+}
+
+func pathCheckCheck(arg1, arg2 ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	pathCheck, ok := arg1.(pathCheckVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	httpRequestVerb, ok := arg2.Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	return pathCheck.Authorize(context.TODO(), httpRequestVerb)
+}
+
+func resourceCheckCheck(arg1, arg2 ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	resourceCheck, ok := arg1.(resourceCheckVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	apiVerb, ok := arg2.Value().(string)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg1)
+	}
+
+	return resourceCheck.Authorize(context.TODO(), apiVerb)
+}
+
+func decisionAllowed(arg ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	decision, ok := arg.(decisionVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg)
+	}
+
+	return types.Bool(decision.authDecision == authorizer.DecisionAllow)
+}
+
+func decisionDenied(arg ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	decision, ok := arg.(decisionVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg)
+	}
+
+	return types.Bool(decision.authDecision == authorizer.DecisionDeny)
+}
+
+func decisionReason(arg ref.Val) ref.Val {
+	decision, ok := arg.(decisionVal)
+	if !ok {
+		return types.MaybeNoSuchOverloadErr(arg)
+	}
+
+	return types.String(decision.reason)
+}
+
+var (
+	AuthorizerType    = cel.ObjectType("kubernetes.authorization.Authorizer")
+	PathCheckType     = cel.ObjectType("kubernetes.authorization.PathCheck")
+	GroupCheckType    = cel.ObjectType("kubernetes.authorization.GroupCheck")
+	ResourceCheckType = cel.ObjectType("kubernetes.authorization.ResourceCheck")
+	DecisionType      = cel.ObjectType("kubernetes.authorization.Decision")
+)
+
+// Resource represents an API resource
+type Resource interface {
+	// GetName returns the name of the object as presented in the request.  On a CREATE operation, the client
+	// may omit name and rely on the server to generate the name.  If that is the case, this method will return
+	// the empty string
+	GetName() string
+	// GetNamespace is the namespace associated with the request (if any)
+	GetNamespace() string
+	// GetResource is the name of the resource being requested.  This is not the kind.  For example: pods
+	GetResource() schema.GroupVersionResource
+	// GetSubresource is the name of the subresource being requested.  This is a different resource, scoped to the parent resource, but it may have a different kind.
+	// For instance, /pods has the resource "pods" and the kind "Pod", while /pods/foo/status has the resource "pods", the sub resource "status", and the kind "Pod"
+	// (because status operates on pods). The binding resource for a pod though may be /pods/foo/binding, which has resource "pods", subresource "binding", and kind "Binding".
+	GetSubresource() string
+}
+
+func NewAuthorizerVal(userInfo user.Info, authorizer authorizer.Authorizer) ref.Val {
+	return authorizerVal{receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(AuthorizerType), userInfo: userInfo, authAuthorizer: authorizer}
+}
+
+func NewResourceAuthorizerVal(userInfo user.Info, authorizer authorizer.Authorizer, requestResource Resource) ref.Val {
+	a := authorizerVal{receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(AuthorizerType), userInfo: userInfo, authAuthorizer: authorizer}
+	resource := requestResource.GetResource()
+	g := a.groupCheck(resource.Group)
+	r := g.resourceCheck(resource.Resource)
+	r.subresource = requestResource.GetSubresource()
+	r.namespace = requestResource.GetNamespace()
+	r.name = requestResource.GetName()
+	return r
+}
+
+type authorizerVal struct {
+	receiverOnlyObjectVal
+	userInfo       user.Info
+	authAuthorizer authorizer.Authorizer
+}
+
+func (a authorizerVal) pathCheck(path string) pathCheckVal {
+	return pathCheckVal{receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(PathCheckType), authorizer: a, path: path}
+}
+
+func (a authorizerVal) groupCheck(group string) groupCheckVal {
+	return groupCheckVal{receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(GroupCheckType), authorizer: a, group: group}
+}
+
+func (a authorizerVal) serviceAccount(namespace, name string) authorizerVal {
+	sa := &serviceaccount.ServiceAccountInfo{Name: name, Namespace: namespace}
+	return authorizerVal{
+		receiverOnlyObjectVal: receiverOnlyVal(AuthorizerType),
+		userInfo:              sa.UserInfo(),
+		authAuthorizer:        a.authAuthorizer,
+	}
+}
+
+type pathCheckVal struct {
+	receiverOnlyObjectVal
+	authorizer authorizerVal
+	path       string
+}
+
+func (a pathCheckVal) Authorize(ctx context.Context, verb string) ref.Val {
+	attr := &authorizer.AttributesRecord{
+		Path: a.path,
+		Verb: verb,
+		User: a.authorizer.userInfo,
+	}
+
+	decision, reason, err := a.authorizer.authAuthorizer.Authorize(ctx, attr)
+	if err != nil {
+		return types.NewErr("error in authorization check: %v", err)
+	}
+	return newDecision(decision, reason)
+}
+
+type groupCheckVal struct {
+	receiverOnlyObjectVal
+	authorizer authorizerVal
+	group      string
+}
+
+func (g groupCheckVal) resourceCheck(resource string) resourceCheckVal {

I think we should limit the number of authz calls, at least to start

something like ~2 max per expression (allows a check and a fallback to a service account or broader/different permission), ~10 per policy?

> I think there's a default deny if every authorizer recuses itself and returns “no opinion”. If that's true, does this code need to handle the no opinion case?

no, it doesn't... we can drop `denied()` from this interface

is this exercised in CI? in alpha clusters with all alpha feature gates enabled, kubelet is panicking on this line:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a1b12e49eac237a37939642d0c3395008b9ab380/pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go#L2412

```
panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference
[signal SIGSEGV: segmentation violation code=0x1 addr=0x18 pc=0x3b5f21f]
k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/kubelet.(*Kubelet).HandlePodAdditions(0xc00073c400, {0xc001816c00?, 0x27, 0x40})
  pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go:2412 +0x4df
```

1. Start a standalone kubelet (not registered to an API server)

2. Manager construction short-circuits `Start()` here:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/253ab3eda71f250ad6692bb16f035cebaf0651c9/pkg/kubelet/status/status_manager.go#L186-L192

3. VPA state is never initialized here:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/253ab3eda71f250ad6692bb16f035cebaf0651c9/pkg/kubelet/status/status_manager.go#L195-L200

4. All pod syncs then panic on this line:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a1b12e49eac237a37939642d0c3395008b9ab380/pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go#L2412

```
panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference
[signal SIGSEGV: segmentation violation code=0x1 addr=0x18 pc=0x3b5f21f]
k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/kubelet.(*Kubelet).HandlePodAdditions(0xc00073c400, {0xc001816c00?, 0x27, 0x40})
  pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go:2412 +0x4df
```

_Originally posted by @liggitt in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/102884#issuecomment-1454089210_

cc @thockin @vinaykul
/sig node
/kind bug
/priority important-soon
looks like this breaks standalone kubelets, opened https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/116262 to track

also, if VPA state cannot be constructed here:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/253ab3eda71f250ad6692bb16f035cebaf0651c9/pkg/kubelet/status/status_manager.go#L195-L200

logging an error, setting state to nil, and continuing means every use of `checkpointState := kl.statusManager.State()` in the pod sync loop is a potential NPE
/assign @vinaykul

If checkpoint state is nil in non-error cases (like standalone kubelet) we can't log errors on every pod sync. We'll flood the error log with these otherwise. 

Is there a way we can make the VPA feature completely inactive/inert for standalone kubelets, rather than sprinkling nil checks throughout, since a kubelet running that way won't be getting pod updates from the API that change resources?
The extra go.mod files are to isolate dependencies used only in test or examples from showing up in the modules used by consumers as libraries

make sure you use `hack/pin-dependency.sh` to update versions and `hack/update-vendor.sh` to rebuild the vendor dir (instructions at https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/vendor.md)

Does the VPA feature make sense for a standalone kubelet? I don't think it does. It would be easier to reason about if standalone kubelets didn't ever hit VPA code paths.
for example, an admission plugin that resolves tags to digests and appends the digest are common... so `busybox:latest` would get turned into `busybox:latest@sha256:...`

/lgtm

> Is this enforced at API today?

Yes, NodeRestriction admission blocks the serviceaccount/secret/configmap/pvc references that would inform node authorizer:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/0270fc75d0f59e1f5a31a810737ac35ac3eeb8a3/plugin/pkg/admission/noderestriction/admission.go#L247-L265

Blocking pod creation there means the node is silently running static pods that *probably* aren't working (API references that require authz fail unless another pod on the same node happens to reference the same API object), and *definitely* are confusing the scheduler because the resources they consume aren't represented in the API.

> Is there any backward compatibility implication to tightening Kubelet validation?

A kubelet could conceivably be running static pods whose referenced resources *happened* to also be referenced by a non-static pod on the same node, but that doesn't seem like it would result in consistently successful pods.

    image: busybox
" > /var/run/kubernetes/static-pods/busybox.yaml

START_MODE=kubeletonly FEATURE_GATES=InPlacePodVerticalScaling=true hack/local-up-cluster.sh
```

Permission to create workloads/deployments/pods in a namespace implicitly lets you mount secrets in that namespace into pods, which can then read those as files. That's why workload/pod creation permissions are grouped with secret read permissions in built-in roles like admin and edit.

This is prominently documented on https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/

> Additionally, anyone who is authorized to create a Pod in a namespace can use that access to read any Secret in that namespace; this includes indirect access such as the ability to create a Deployment.

In theory, cluster admins *could* put controls in place to only allow specific authorized images / commands to be run, or reject pods/exec permissions, in order to try to allow users to create pods without accessing secrets in the namespace, but in general, ability to create pods in a namespace is only expected to be given to users who have significant permissions within that namespace.
I don't think this is a good idea... it means you can typo an exclude, think you are not running a controller, your typo gets ignored and the controller you meant to exclude keeps running

the answer is that a call to Watch() that is establishing a new watcher could be in this section of code, where it has already passed the `c.ready.wait` check, but has not yet registered the new watcher in a way that `terminateAllWatchers()` would catch and remove:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f90643435ef606afbd156490933b200479c34e2d/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go#L524-L623

it might be helpful to expand this comment with something like:
```
Watchers currently being established might not be terminated by this call, so we need to call again under lock.
Watchers we terminate here who try to re-establish themselves will block until the ready signal.
This is just an optimization, the authoritative termination before going ready is inside the SetOnReplace function.
```
I don't think it matters for correctness since we're holding the lock here, but terminating existing watchers *before* marking ready makes more logical sense

-	w.startIndex = 0
-	w.endIndex = 0
+	// We have to ensure that startIndex is never decreased.
+	// Previously we were resetting startIndex to 0 and that was exposing
+	// us to the following race:
+	// - a new Watch was registered, and watchCacheInterval is returned for it
+	//   (but it didn't yet started processing the interval)
+	// - Cacher is being reinitialized (which terminates this watcher, but

+	// - watchCache is receiving new events which start to being written
+	//   starting at index 0
+	// - now the Watch from the first point starts actual processing - it

+	//   overwritten indexes, it will return bad data (from the future
+	//   which then may be followed by past data for indexes which weren't

+	default:
+		return nil, fmt.Errorf("%q is not a valid node selector operator", nsr.Operator)
+	}

/lgtm

Thanks for all the iterations, the test coverage on this is really good

/priority important-soon

> But then how would kubelet learn about the right ResourceClaim? Can it watch "all" ResourceClaims with an informer and will it then only receive those that it has access to, if node authorizer checks that? I suppose that must work, because kubelet also needs to be informed efficiently about pods that were scheduled onto its node.

Pods are not narrowly authorized, actually... any node can list/watch all pods, they just happen to filter with a field selector to just the pods they care about. Field selectors are not in the set of information considered by authorization.

Pods are also a special case for scaling watch... they have a bespoke watch indexer hard-coded into the apiserver to make filtering by node name performant. I would not assume thousands of nodes watching all resource claims filtering by a label would perform well generically.

If the name can be deterministic, that would be much better both for the kubelet discovering and authorizing.

> when it can read/write any pod

it can only write pods assigned to it, it can read any pod

> Suppose the node authorizer was modified to do authorization for ResourceClaim by label.

Authorizers don't have access to label selectors on incoming requests. Xref discussion in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/44703#issuecomment-324826356

> Would a watch then a) send only authorized objects

A watch could send objects that only matched a label selector, but authorization couldn't force a specific label selector

> and b) would that be efficient?

A cluster-wide watch that sets a label selector that matches a fraction of the existing entries is not efficient. Every node starting a watch like this on an API that is high volume and fast moving (potentially 1:1 with pods) sounds problematic
it would probably be good to add redirects on the website as well to make the old URLs continue working - https://github.com/kubernetes/website/blob/main/static/_redirects

* ResourceClaimName is valid (passes the same validation we apply in ValidateClaim)
* ResourceClaimTemplateName is valid (passes the same validation we apply in ValidateClaimTemplate)

From what we can tell, *something* in the VPA path (`computePodResizeAction `) thought a static pod's resources needed to trigger the resize code path.

I don't know enough about static pod handling to know if that is a category error and VPA should *never* expect to resize resources used by static pods. > the SSA client code there uses standard JSON marshalling of the object

that's not the correct way to make a SSA patch... it includes zero-values for too many fields, not just creationTimestamp

https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/tree/master/applyconfigurations is the library to use for making SSA patches for standard types

as an example, https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/blob/master/applyconfigurations/apps/v1/deployment.go#L39-L63 can extract fields from a Deployment object to make an SSA patch, or construct a patch from scratch

>  ideally "The set of resources which need to be read in order to run a pod are directly linked by the pod". Good principle.

Yeah, I like that. Either deterministic mappings or explicit references from pod spec/status work in my view.

Putting the name of the spawned claim(s) in pod status for kubelets to discover and for the node authorizer to authorize on *could* work. `NodeRestriction` admission would need an update to disallow nodes adding names of claims in pod status, but that's not hard.

I don't have a good sense for the trust level we're placing in the controller(s) handling these claim objects and whether we'd be happy letting those controller(s) write to status of arbitrary pods. I haven't followed the design enough to know if we expect a single central controller running on the control plane (which we'd be happy to let write pod status), or if this is a distributed bring-your-own-controller model (where we would *not* want to hand out pod status update permissions like candy).
sorry, I meant the controller creating the resourceclaim object and updating pod status (so the node and node authorizer could allow / get the claim object)

> Do you mean that for every custom type that I'd want to use SSA with I should implement something similar to that

yes, it's included in the client-go generators alongside standard client/informer/lister generators - https://github.com/kubernetes/code-generator/tree/master/cmd/applyconfiguration-gen

if controller-runtime is creating SSA patches by json-serializing standard API types, they're doing it wrong

oof... this means we were actually updating the pod with a condition as part of handling a dry-run evict previously? Actually, this may be able to be simpler.

If we have a resourceVersion precondition in deleteoptions, copy pod and set the resourceVersion from deleteOptions into the pod. That will make the Update call honor the resourceVersion precondition.
we should not be re-getting the pod here if we already got it in EvictionREST#Create and did logic based on it... the pod we're updating/deleting should be passed in

mutating the input options is not something I'd recommend... work with a copy of deleteOptions and update it locally only.

if we need to propagate a resource version change to other invocations of this method (which I'm not sure we do), return it as a return value instead of mutating input variables

something like:

1. when we're calling from a place we already have the current pod in EvictionREST#Create, pass in the pod
2. when we did not already fetch the pod, pass in nil
3. in this function, only call getPod() if the passed-in pod is nil

if !dryrun.IsDryRun(deleteOptionsCopy.DryRun) && feature.DefaultFeatureGate.Enabled(features.PodDisruptionConditions) {

```

There are three modes the kubelet can be in:
1. never talk to an API server (kubeClient is nil)
2. talk to an API server it is responsible for starting (kubeClient is non-nil, but API calls will fail until the kubelet starts its static pods)
3. talk to an API server someone else is responsible for starting (kubeClient is non-nil, API calls will ~eventually work if the kubelet just keeps on trying)

This PR is working to fix scenario 1.

Separately, in scenario 2, does `updateReconstructedDevicePaths()` block the kubelet from running the kube-apiserver container? If so, is that different from what the previous volume reconstruction did?

for review of standalone behavior here

> I'm hesitant and risk-averse to just swap out `pod` with `mirrorPod` so close to code freeze.

Agreed on not making big changes right before code-freeze. Can you open a tracking issue for that and tag @smarterclayton @bobbypage on it?

> This is why I feel it is best to disable static pods for resize at this time and do it right in 1.28

As commented in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/116504#discussion_r1134868462, it seems like the static pod carve-outs and feature gate checks are leaving gaps in that series of `if` statements relative to 1.26 handling event when the feature is disabled... at the very least, it's really hard to reason about, which is not what we want from feature-gated isolated code

I think that PR needs to be made more obviously isolated / no-op when the feature gate is disabled, or we should consider dropping VPA out of 1.27
/approve

Yeah... "Vars will be removed in future release" sounds ... like it will be removed in a future release :-)

> There's been a warning printed since kustomize v4.5.6

That's helpful to know, at least the previous versions of kubectl would have warned about this.

I actually looked back and the `patches` string field was already marked as deprecated in the first version of kustomize added to kubectl, which is good.

> IIRC from when we made the removal decision, it originally had the type []string, and the string values could either be file paths or inline SMPs. Next, it was renamed to patchesStrategicMerge, which still exists and still has those same semantics. I'm not sure whether the patches field itself was briefly been removed at that point or not, but in any case I bet it would have been in a v3-series version that didn't ever go into kubectl. At any rate, it was soon after re-introduced with different syntax and semantics, i.e. [the ones it has today](https://kubectl.docs.kubernetes.io/references/kustomize/kustomization/patches/).

That's is... rather unfortunate. Hopefully there are tests in place now (like the cross-version compat tests Kubernetes has at https://github.com/kubernetes/api/tree/master/testdata) to keep that sort of evolution from occurring again in the future.

The response body of successful write requests is reserved for returning the updated object, so side-channel information cannot be added to it compatibly.

That limits side-channel information to headers. The `Warning` header is a standard header, which means we have more confidence intermediate proxies will propagate it (or at least not break when encountering it), and has well-specified semantics that include explicitly forbidding taking automated action based on the content.

It's not clear that another header-based non-API side channel is needed or worth the effort to add, especially if it would require using a non-standard header.

`audience` controls the content of the `aud` claim in the token, it doesn't change the generator of the token. 
 serviceAccountTokens are always generated by the kubernetes signer/key.

Given this token is a service account token, it is expected it was issued by the kubernetes signer.

/close

In the case you describe, if warnings are not appropriate for the information you are wanting to display, I would consider annotating the object or adding a condition or emitting an event that is API visible, rather than trying to add another informative side-channel

> changing how unwanted dependencies are detected

I don't think I agree with this change... `github.com/distribution/distribution/v3` does actually add more paths to unwanted dependencies:

```
go mod graph | grep github.com/gorilla/mux
github.com/distribution/distribution/v3@v3.0.0-20230223072852-e5d5810851d1 github.com/gorilla/mux@v1.8.0
```

Dependency pruning is nice to make less stuff get fetched to build a given snapshot, but we _really_ don't want these unwanted dependencies in our graph _at all_.

The fact that we don't *happen* to currently depend on a package that pulls them into our build isn't enough. We're only one package import away (either in this repo or in the dependency's repo where we have less visibility/control) from pulling in the unwanted source, and the transitive dependencies of the pruned-but-unwanted dependency still inform the module graph and minimum version selection.

comment_text:
thanks for the PR, but the methods in this package fall into two categories:
1. getters for specific fields which apply to all objects (fields in metadata, apiVersion/kind fields, etc)
2. helpers to get typed values for arbitrary fields whose names are passed as parameters

status.replicas / status.availableReplicas / status.readyReplicas are not generic fields, so getters for those don't really belong in this package (and can already be gotten using calls to the existing methods like `NestedInt64(u.Object, "status", "replicas")`, etc)

The copy from root to leaf is generally required to make sure we end up with consistent versions referenced

> If a staging go.mod file has an indirect dependency in its go.mod file but the root go.mod does not

I think this is the key thing we want to resolve. Before we copy the root go.mod to staging modules, we want it to be an aggregated go.mod that contains *all* the dependencies and versions from *all* our modules (root + staging)... that's what ensure_require_replace_directives_for_all_dependencies was trying to accomplish.

We then copy that to all the staging repos, and tidy all.

there's an existing issue open for this: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/107414

In 1.9, this was stored in etcd as v1alpha1: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.9/test/integration/etcd/etcd_storage_path_test.go#L300-L305

In 1.10, this was stored in etcd as v1beta1, but existing objects in etcd could still be read using v1alpha1 from then until now: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.10/test/integration/etcd/etcd_storage_path_test.go#L306-L319

Since we don't know if someone upgraded through those versions and still has persisted data in v1alpha1 format, we have to retain the ability to read that data

according to godoc:

> Bytes returns a slice of length b.Len() holding the unread portion of the buffer. The slice is valid for use only until the next buffer modification (that is, only until the next call to a method like Read, Write, Reset, or Truncate)

My read of the godoc is that the bytes returned from the buffer are unsafe to use without copying, which largely negates the point of reusing the buffer.

I think we should remove the TODO rather than try to reuse the buffer here.

Reset() puts the starting point back to 0, but retains the previously allocated memory to write into:

> Reset resets the buffer to be empty, but it retains the underlying storage for use by future writes. Reset is the same as Truncate(0).

I don't think this makes it safe to write to a buffer we previously returned a byte slice from
/hold

/approve

> I fetched the latest code of the master branch

did you fetch with `--tags`? what does `git describe` output for you? it should show something like `v1.28.0-alpha.0-25-g2c281710311`

> I was in an impression that, once an API has been marked as deprecated, we are also bailing out from the guarantee that, those API versions are no longer available to be read/retrieve.

Definitely not. From https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/using-api/deprecation-policy/:

> Note: Until [#52185](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/52185) is resolved, no API versions that have been persisted to storage may be removed. Serving REST endpoints for those versions may be disabled (subject to the deprecation timelines in this document), but the API server must remain capable of decoding/converting previously persisted data from storage.

> It looks like it's not the case rather once a version made into storage, it's always available to get it. Am I correct on this understanding?

Until we have some form of guaranteed storage migration, that's correct. The only point at which the alpha type could have been dropped unconditionally was in the release when the beta was introduced, and that would have generated an "action required" release note that all existing alpha objects would have to be deleted before upgrading. Now that we've allowed alpha / beta / v1 data to mix in etcd, we should not break the ability to read / convert the alpha objects.

> Considering we have to support the ability to read data, will bringing back the conversion functions (pkg/apis/storage/v1alpha1/zz_generated.conversion.go) of v1alpha satisfy the requirement ? or there are more changes required ?

You need the types.go (to decode the v1alpha1 objects), defaults.go (to apply v1alpha1 defaults), and conversion (to translate it into the internal versions). Essentially all the code removed in this PR.

A couple things I noticed in your example:
* Kubernetes does not use tokens stored in Secret API objects for mounting into pods any more, it generates tokens on the fly and injects them, so whatever you do with Secret API objects has nothing to do with tokens that end up inside the pod. If you look at the pod that is running, you should see a projectedToken volume unless you opted out of mounting the token in with `automountServiceAccountToken: false` on the pod or the serviceaccount.
* The `kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets` annotation on the secret isn't accomplishing anything, that annotation belongs on the ServiceAccount if you want to limit mounted Secret objects to the ones named in the service account's `secrets` list

there's two pieces around the migration:

DeepCopy *does* copy priorityClassName. Non-pointer fields in PodSpec are copied here: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/565fd4116df866a642e245d6ef9c2fe94448b265/staging/src/k8s.io/api/core/v1/zz_generated.deepcopy.go#L3915

kubectl writes an entire object at a time to the output stream, so you could reasonably accumulate lines into a buffer and assume an object is complete if you either see `---` or if no new lines are added after some small time interval, but I can see how that would be fiddly.

if you're piping kubectl watch output to something that will process streaming invocations, and want completely deterministic parsing, I'd suggest using `-o json`

neither of the following would be correct for kubectl to do:
* output `---` when it doesn't know if there will be another object written
* output `...` when it doesn't know if there will be another object written

This does not seem like a Kubernetes bug. Log content is not an API we guarantee, and downstreams taking hard dependencies on exact log content seems like a problem / anti-pattern we want to flush out and get rid of, not enable.

/lgtm
/approve

I updated the release note to remove the reference to consistency with controller-runtime / API standards, since neither of those are things that we promise log output will match

I'm on the fence about picking to 1.27.1... I could go either way

/remove-kind bug
/kind cleanup
/remove-kind failing-test

If the kubelet killed the container because of active deadline seconds, then Failed status makes sense, but the right way for a user to match against this is not by matching exitCode 0, but by some positive indication the kubelet killed the container.

If the deadline and the container exiting normally raced, and the kubelet _didn't_ actually kill the container, and it exited with 0, I don't think we should mark it Failed.

I have a hard time seeing this fall within the guidelines for cherry-picks (https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/devel/sig-release/cherry-picks.md#what-kind-of-prs-are-good-for-cherry-picks)

It's not a critical bug fix, and it's not an obviously safe change – expanding an interface, some tricky wiring/plumbing of metrics initialization (which actually bit us [in master](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/115326)), and registering additional metrics by default
dependency update mechanics look good, a couple questions about ebpf init() functions, and would like an ack from a node approver on the content of the updates

> @liggitt btw, I've noticed some unsuccessful attempts to enable dependabot for k/k, e.g. #113371. Any thoughts/objections about enabling it at some point?

I have mixed feelings:
* we have at least some dependencies that are poorly behaved (e.g. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/107499), and dependabot will push us to versions that break
* dependabot won't understand our scripts to manage dependencies for multiple modules, and will open PRs that just update go.mod files in ways that won't actually work

/lgtm
/approve

+	AcceptV2Beta1Params = sortedContentTypeParams{

+		},

+	// Aggregated discovery content-type (v2beta1). NOTE: content-type parameters
+	// MUST be ordered (g, v, as) for server in "Accept" header (BUT we are resilient
+	// to ordering when comparing returned values in "Content-Type" header).

+	// Prioritize aggregated discovery by placing first in the order of discovery accept types.

+)
+
+// sortedContentTypeParams encapsulated content type parameters, where
+// the discovery content-type parameters will be ordered when printed,
+// but where parameter ordering will not matter when comparing.
+type sortedContentTypeParams struct {
+	params map[string]string
+}
+
+// sortedKeys returns content-type parameters sorted so keys
+// "g" > "v" > "as". No deterministic ordering if keys are not
+// discovery content-type parameters {"g", "v", "as"}.
+func (p *sortedContentTypeParams) sortedKeys() []string {
+	keys := make([]string, len(p.params))
+	for key := range p.params {
+		keys = append(keys, key)
+	}
+	// Ordering: "g" > "v" > "as"
+	sort.Slice(keys, func(i, j int) bool {
+		if strings.EqualFold(keys[i], "g") {
+			return true
+		} else if strings.EqualFold(keys[i],"as") {
+			return false
+		} else if strings.EqualFold(keys[i], "v") && strings.EqualFold(keys[j], "as") {
+			return true
+		} else {
+			return false
+		}
+	})
+	return keys
+}
+
+// String prints the content-type params using the sortedKeys array to
+// print the params in the order of the "sortedKeys" field.
+func (p *sortedContentTypeParams) String() string {
+	keyValues := []string{}
+	for _, key := range p.sortedKeys() {
+		if value, ok := p.params[key]; ok {
+			keyValues = append(keyValues, key+"="+value)
+		}
+	}
+	return strings.Join(keyValues, ";")
+}
+
+// Equals returns true if the passed parameters map is the same as the
+// receivers parameters. NOTE: the ordering of the parametes does NOT matter.
+func (p *sortedContentTypeParams) Equals(params map[string]string) bool {
+	return reflect.DeepEqual(p.params, params)

switch {

+See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
+limitations under the License.
+*/
+
+package discovery
+
+import (
+	"mime"
+	"reflect"
+	"sort"
+	"strings"
+
+	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime"
+)
+
+var (
+	AcceptV1            = runtime.ContentTypeJSON
+	AcceptV2Beta1Params = sortedContentTypeParams{
+		params: map[string]string{
+			"g":  "apidiscovery.k8s.io",
+			"v":  "v2beta1",
+			"as": "APIGroupDiscoveryList",
+		},
+	}
+	// Aggregated discovery content-type (v2beta1). NOTE: content-type parameters
+	// MUST be ordered (g, v, as) for server in "Accept" header (BUT we are resilient
+	// to ordering when comparing returned values in "Content-Type" header).
+	AcceptV2Beta1 = runtime.ContentTypeJSON + ";" + AcceptV2Beta1Params.String()
+	// Prioritize aggregated discovery by placing first in the order of discovery accept types.
+	acceptDiscoveryFormats = AcceptV2Beta1 + "," + AcceptV1
+)
+
+// sortedContentTypeParams encapsulated content type parameters, where
+// the discovery content-type parameters will be ordered when printed,
+// but where parameter ordering will not matter when comparing.
+type sortedContentTypeParams struct {
+	params map[string]string
+}
+
+// sortedKeys returns content-type parameters sorted so keys
+// "g" > "v" > "as". No deterministic ordering if keys are not
+// discovery content-type parameters {"g", "v", "as"}.
+func (p *sortedContentTypeParams) sortedKeys() []string {
+	keys := make([]string, len(p.params))
+	for key := range p.params {
+		keys = append(keys, key)
+	}
+	// Ordering: "g" > "v" > "as"
+	sort.Slice(keys, func(i, j int) bool {
+		if strings.EqualFold(keys[i], "g") {
+			return true
+		} else if strings.EqualFold(keys[i],"as") {
+			return false
+		} else if strings.EqualFold(keys[i], "v") && strings.EqualFold(keys[j], "as") {
+			return true
+		} else {
+			return false
+		}
+	})
+	return keys
+}
+
+// String prints the content-type params using the sortedKeys array to
+// print the params in the order of the "sortedKeys" field.
+func (p *sortedContentTypeParams) String() string {
+	keyValues := []string{}
+	for _, key := range p.sortedKeys() {
+		if value, ok := p.params[key]; ok {
+			keyValues = append(keyValues, key+"="+value)
+		}
+	}
+	return strings.Join(keyValues, ";")
+}
+
+// Equals returns true if the passed parameters map is the same as the
+// receivers parameters. NOTE: the ordering of the parametes does NOT matter.
+func (p *sortedContentTypeParams) Equals(params map[string]string) bool {
+	return reflect.DeepEqual(p.params, params)
+}
+
+// IsV1ContentType returns true if the passed content-type string is
+// discovery version 1 (e.g. "application/json"). NOTE: The content-type
+// parameters can be anything OTHER than v2beta1 parameters.
+// Examples:
+//
+//	"application/json" = true
+//	"application/json; charset=UTF-8" = true
+//	"application/json; g=apidiscovery.k8s.io;v=v2beta1;as=APIGroupDiscoveryList" = false
+func IsV1ContentType(contentType string) bool {
+	base, params, err := mime.ParseMediaType(contentType)
+	if err != nil {
+		return false
+	}
+	return AcceptV1 == base && !AcceptV2Beta1Params.Equals(params)
+}
+
+// IsV2Beta1ContentType checks of the content-type string is both
+// "application/json" and contains the v2beta1 content-type params.
+// NOTE: This function is resilient to the ordering of the
+// content-type parameters. Examples:
+//
+//	"application/json; g=apidiscovery.k8s.io;v=v2beta1;as=APIGroupDiscoveryList" = true
+//	"application/json; as=APIGroupDiscoveryList;v=v2beta1;g=apidiscovery.k8s.io" = true
+//	"application/json" = false
+//	"application/json; charset=UTF-8" = false
+func IsV2Beta1ContentType(contentType string) bool {
+	base, params, err := mime.ParseMediaType(contentType)
+	if err != nil {
+		return false
+	}
+	return AcceptV1 == base && AcceptV2Beta1Params.Equals(params)

comment_text:
Today, what the server returns is correct for standard server configurations, and incorrect for proxied+prefixed configurations.

Making the client assume the server returns incorrect information and unconditionally add in its own prefix limits what we can do on the server side to improve this in the future.

I would probably make this change conditional on `ServerRelativeURL` starting with `/openapi/v3/`. If `ServerRelativeURL` starts with something else, then treat it as an actual server-relative URL and continue using `RequestURI`. That would let us improve things on the server side in the future and not break clients containing this fix.

mm... that's a good call... I think the thing constructing the groupversion should have more context:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/0b53923a7a02243da2fc62be0889ef8ed8d462e0/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/openapi/client.go#L42-L61

There, we're requesting /openapi/v3, so we could decide at that point whether the server returned something aware of a different context root and indicate whether we want to preserve the client-side prefix or use the exact path:

```diff
diff --git a/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/openapi/client.go b/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/openapi/client.go
index 7b58762acfd..9fca751a5cd 100644

-		result[k] = newGroupVersion(c, v)
+		// If the server returned a URL rooted at /openapi/v3, preserve any additional client-side prefix.
+		// If the server returned a URL not rooted at /openapi/v3, treat it as an actual server-relative URL.
+		// See https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/117463 for details
+		useClientPrefix := strings.HasPrefix(v.ServerRelativeURL, "/openapi/v3")
+		result[k] = newGroupVersion(c, v, useClientPrefix)
 	}
 	return result, nil
 }

+)
+
+func TestGroupVersion(t *testing.T) {
+	tests := []struct {
+		name      string
+		prefix    string
+		openapiV3 bool
+	}{
+		{
+			name:      "openapiv3 endpoint without prefix as standard server configuration",
+			openapiV3: true,
+			prefix:    "",
+		},
+		{
+			name:      "openapiv3 endpoint with prefix which is for proxied connections",
+			prefix:    "/test-endpoint",
+			openapiV3: true,
+		},
+		{
+			name:      "openapiv2 endpoint without prefix as standard server configuration",
+			prefix:    "",
+			openapiV3: false,
+		},
+		{
+			name:      "openapiv2 endpoint with prefix which is for proxied connections",
+			prefix:    "/test-endpoint",
+			openapiV3: false,
+		},
+	}
+
