+	"k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes/scheme"
+	"k8s.io/client-go/rest"
+)
+
+func TestGroupVersion(t *testing.T) {
+	tests := []struct {
+		name      string
+		prefix    string
+		openapiV3 bool
+	}{
+		{
+			name:      "openapiv3 endpoint without prefix as standard server configuration",
+			openapiV3: true,
+			prefix:    "",
+		},
+		{
+			name:      "openapiv3 endpoint with prefix which is for proxied connections",
+			prefix:    "/test-endpoint",
+			openapiV3: true,
+		},
+		{
+			name:      "openapiv2 endpoint without prefix as standard server configuration",
+			prefix:    "",
+			openapiV3: false,
+		},
+		{
+			name:      "openapiv2 endpoint with prefix which is for proxied connections",
+			prefix:    "/test-endpoint",
+			openapiV3: false,
+		},
+	}
+
+	for _, test := range tests {
+		t.Run(test.name, func(t *testing.T) {
+			server := httptest.NewServer(http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
+				switch {
+				case r.URL.Path == test.prefix+"/openapi/v3/apis/apps/v1" && r.URL.RawQuery == "hash=014fbff9a07c":
+					w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
+					w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
+					w.Write([]byte(`{"openapi":"3.0.0","info":{"title":"Kubernetes","version":"unversioned"}}`))
+				case r.URL.Path == "/openapi/v2/apis/apps/v1" && r.URL.RawQuery == "hash=014fbff9a07c":
+					w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
+					w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
+					w.Write([]byte(`{"openapi":"2.0.0","info":{"title":"Kubernetes","version":"unversioned"}}`))
+				case r.URL.Path == test.prefix+"/openapi/v3":
+					// return root content
+					w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
+					w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
+					if test.openapiV3 {
+						w.Write([]byte(`{"paths":{"apis/apps/v1":{"serverRelativeURL":"/openapi/v3/apis/apps/v1?hash=014fbff9a07c"}}}`))
+					} else {
+						w.Write([]byte(`{"paths":{"apis/apps/v1":{"serverRelativeURL":"/openapi/v2/apis/apps/v1?hash=014fbff9a07c"}}}`))
+					}
+				default:
+					t.Errorf("unexpected request: %s", r.URL.String())
+					w.WriteHeader(http.StatusNotFound)
+					return
+				}
+			}))
+			defer server.Close()
+
+			c, err := rest.RESTClientFor(&rest.Config{
+				Host: server.URL + test.prefix,
+				ContentConfig: rest.ContentConfig{
+					NegotiatedSerializer: scheme.Codecs,
+					GroupVersion:         &appsv1.SchemeGroupVersion,
+				},
+			})
+
+			openapiClient := NewClient(c)
+			paths, err := openapiClient.Paths()
+			if err != nil {
+				t.Fatalf("unexpected error occurred: %v", err)
+			}
+			schema, err := paths["apis/apps/v1"].Schema(runtime.ContentTypeJSON)
+			if err != nil {
+				t.Fatalf("unexpected error occurred: %v", err)
+			}
+			expectedResult := `{"openapi":"3.0.0","info":{"title":"Kubernetes","version":"unversioned"}}`
+			if !test.openapiV3 {
+				expectedResult = `{"openapi":"2.0.0","info":{"title":"Kubernetes","version":"unversioned"}}`
+			}

comment_text: curious why this is gated... this is essentially a bugfix, right? the current order breaks https://kubernetes.io/releases/version-skew-policy/#kube-proxy
having a way to opt out for a release if there are issues maybe seems ok, but shouldn't this default _on_ so kubeadm doesn't continue breaking supported skew by default?

another way we've approached changing undesirable/buggy behaviors is to gate them with a `Deprecated` feature gate, default it to false, and name it in a way that describes the unwanted behavior, so `UpgradeAddonsBeforeControlPlane`, state Deprecated, and default false, then mark the deprecated gate to be removed in a couple releases

ideas for naming:
* describing the use might be clearer? ValidateStoredExpressions?
* this env allows all known cel features / functions - latest? ...?

if we keep track of the major/minor this envset should be compatible with, then Extend / ExtendProgram can take the major/minor along with the option and decide whether to add the given option to `Supported` or not

then consumers could use the same method to compatibly add things to their envs:

```
admissionEnv.Extend({1,28}, ... some option...)
admissionEnv.Extend({1,32}, ... some option...)
```

comment_text: why are we enqueuing the oldPod in addition to newPod when the labels are equal?
this adds a typo, and stringification of labels is expensive

logically, it looks like the only thing we care about here are the labels.

I wonder if three queue levels would make more sense:
1. process pods
  a. add their labels to a map[namespace]{podName, podNamespace, labels}
  b. queue their namespace for processing
2. process namespaces
  a. grab all the sets of labels observed for that namespace
  b. resolve to covering pdbs (logging using the podName/podNamespace if there are multiple pdbs found or no pdbs found)
  c. queue the pdbs for processing
3. process pdbs

as an aside, processing pod-by-pod assumes we will see every pod event... on startup, it seems like we have to do an initial update of all pdbs to cover any pod events we missed... are we doing that?
this was removed in 1.27 and can be re-enabled for one release past that, so this should hold until 1.29
Adding `json:"-"` would be a simpler way to resolve the linked issue

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind feature

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Currently, versions are baked into component-base binaries at build time using ldflags, and any change to any portion of the version (even prerelease / buildID portions) requires building new binaries.

This PR allows `--version=v1.x.y...` to override the prelease/buildID portion of the reported version. To avoid confusion about build content, _only_ the prerelease/buildID portion of the version can be overridden. The major/minor/patch, git SHA, and build time all remain fixed.

This enables release workflows like the following:
* a release candidate build can be promoted without needing to be rebuilt
  * The qualified SHA can have the final release tag added to it, and the already-qualified release candidate binaries can be run and told to identify themselves as v1.x.y-$buildID (with the SHA they advertise matching the tag added in git).
  * Promotion flows along these lines have been discussed in the past (e.g. https://kubernetes.slack.com/archives/CJH2GBF7Y/p1605215765446100?thread_ts=1605208348.437200&cid=CJH2GBF7Y, cc @tpepper @justaugustus)
    * > tpepper: Release then is a promotion of a candidate and each candidate is aimed to be fully ready as-is at the binary level.  The “trick” would be in not having any pre-release builds binary variants “visible” in normal consumption paths and having clear signs in the binary (eg: the so-called “gitVersion”?) so that it’s readily apparent which build you’re using.
    * > justaugustus: I love having a stub of the git version in there for tracking things down. a buildVersion could be cool, to signify that it was build from CI or something. and only gets populated on a CI build. Allowing this option could be cool for others building Kubernetes outside of our processes
    * > tpepper: that’s my long term dream outcome….build things, test them, if good, then release them (as opposed to build something new and release it)
* component binaries included in larger bundles can be told their user-facing buildID at runtime
  * a bundle like this could include inputs beyond the Kubernetes binaries which could change without the Kubernetes binaries needing to be rebuilt

This is immediately useful for things building on component-base, and could be used in the future for streamlining the Kubernetes release process for a qualified release candidate.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
`--version=v1.X.Y...` can now be used to set the prerelease and buildID portions of the version reported by components
```

for visibility to release possibilities:
/sig release

this was an instance of the problems with "derived defaults" described in https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#static-defaults:

> In some cases, these values may be conditional on or deterministically derived from other fields (e.g. "if otherField is X then this field defaults to 0" or "this field defaults to the value of otherField"). Note that such derived defaults present a hazard in the face of updates - if the "other" field changes, the derived field may have to change, too. The static defaulting logic is unaware of updates and has no concept of "previous value", which means this inter-field relationship becomes the user's problem - they must update both the field they care about and the "other" field.

so changing it (by moving the defaulting to the pod, which is immutable on update) seems useful if we can swing it

The immediately mechanical issue I see is that it looks like validation *requires* hostPort == containerPort when hostNetwork == true, even at the deployment, etc, level:
   * ValidatePodTemplateSpec → ValidatePodSpec → ValidatePodSecurityContext → validateHostNetwork: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f871d5fabedf346c5db694b726be6dddfd48fa2f/pkg/apis/core/validation/validation.go#L3484-L3492
   * How is this validation passing on deployments that set `hostNetwork: true` but rely on defaulting to populate hostPort in all the container ports? Do we not have tests for that?

More esoteric implications of this:
1. a client that sends Update requests for deployments, etc, will no longer get hostPort fields defaulted inside containers. That means an update to an object persisted in a previous release you would expect to be a no-op will now trigger a generation change and rollout, even though the resulting pods would be identical.
2. pod-creating controllers (replicaset, job, cronjob, statefulset, daemonset) have to tolerate defaulting differences between the pod-spawning object and the pods without triggering rollouts to try to make the pods *exactly* match the workload spec. I *think* that already has to be generally true since we already have pod-specific defaulting, but I've been surprised before, which led to https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/78914, which I don't think we've ever shored up this aspect to verify controllers are well-behaved.

is marking pod processing as _failed_ really how we mark as unprocessed? I would have hoped we could indicate reprocessing was needed in a way that didn't indicate failure.

also, this isn't indicating which volumes specifically need reprocessing, right? will this trigger extra work or cause problems for non-configmap/secret volumes in the pod?
cc @wojtek-t for visibility to changes here

this closure taking objectCache-level locks and modifying a local map is pretty hard to reason about ... is there a way we can layer in managing item → pod updates without making the watch_based_manager pod-specific or embedding state in closures like this?
having a way to observe changes to namespace/name pairs seems ok, but making this interface pod-specific doesn't seem great... can the item → pod tracking be done as a separate layer?
I'm really unsure if this is the right place to hook this... cc @bobbypage @smarterclayton for visibility to kubelet / pod / storage flows

@@ -0,0 +1,249 @@
+/*
+Copyright 2023 The Kubernetes Authors.
+
+Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
+you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
+You may obtain a copy of the License at
+
+    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
+
+Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
+distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
+WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
+See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
+limitations under the License.
+*/
+
+package environment
+
+import (
+	"fmt"
+
+	"github.com/google/cel-go/cel"
+
+	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/version"
+	apiservercel "k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/cel"
+)
+
+// Type defines the different types of CEL environments used in Kubernetes.
+// CEL environments are used to compile and evaluate CEL expressions.
+// Environments include:
+//   - Function libraries
+//   - Variables
+//   - Types (both core CEL types and Kubernetes types)
+//   - Other CEL environment and program options
+type Type string
+
+const (
+	// ExpressionValidation is used to validate new or modified expressions in
+	// requests that write expressions to API resources.
+	//
+	// This environment type is compatible with a specific Kubernetes
+	// major/minor version. To ensure safe rollback, this environment type
+	// may not include all the function libraries, variables, type declarations, and CEL
+	// language settings available in the ExpressionStorage environment type.
+	//
+	// ExpressionValidation must be used to validate (parse, compile, type check)
+	// all new or modified CEL expressions before they are written to storage.
+	ExpressionValidation Type = "ExpressionValidation"
+
+	// ExpressionStorage is used to compile and run CEL expressions that have been
+	// persisted to storage.
+	//
+	// This environment type is compatible with CEL expressions that have been
+	// persisted to storage by any version of Kubernetes. This is the most
+	// permissive environment available.
+	//
+	// ExpressionStorage is appropriate for use with CEL expressions in
+	// configuration files.
+	ExpressionStorage Type = "ExpressionStorage"
+)
+
+// EnvSet manages the creation and extension of CEL environments. Each EnvSet contains
+// both an ExpressionValidation and ExpressionStorage environment. EnvSets are created
+// and extended using VersionedOptions so that the EnvSet can prepare environments according
+// to what options were introduced at which versions.
+//
+// Each EnvSet is given a compatibility version when it is created, and prepares the
+// ExpressionValidation environment to be compatible with that version. The EnvSet also
+// prepares ExpressionStorage to be compatible with all known versions of Kubernetes.
+type EnvSet struct {
+	// compatibilityVersion is the version that all configuration in
+	// the ExpressionValidation environment is compatible with.
+	compatibilityVersion *version.Version
+
+	// expressionValidation is an environment containing only configuration
+	// in this EnvSet that is enabled at this compatibilityVersion.
+	expressionValidation *cel.Env
+
+	// expressionStorage is an environment containing the latest configuration
+	// in this EnvSet.
+	expressionStorage *cel.Env
+}
+
+func newEnvSet(compatibilityVersion *version.Version, opts []VersionedOptions) (*EnvSet, error) {
+	base, err := cel.NewEnv()
+	if err != nil {
+		return nil, err
+	}
+	baseSet := EnvSet{compatibilityVersion: compatibilityVersion, expressionValidation: base, expressionStorage: base}
+	return baseSet.Extend(opts...)
+}
+
+func mustNewEnvSet(ver *version.Version, opts []VersionedOptions) *EnvSet {
+	envSet, err := newEnvSet(ver, opts)
+	if err != nil {
+		panic(fmt.Sprintf("Default environment misconfigured: %v", err))
+	}
+	return envSet
+}
+
+// Env returns the CEL environment for the given Type.
+func (e *EnvSet) Env(envType Type) *cel.Env {
+	switch envType {
+	case ExpressionValidation:
+		return e.expressionValidation
+	case ExpressionStorage:
+		return e.expressionStorage
+	default:
+		panic(fmt.Sprintf("Unsupported environment type: %v", envType))

This name is pretty misleading, since this doesn't actually verify the whole security context, only the hostnetwork port bits, and relies on validatePodSpecSecurityContext being called separately. With a single caller, I'd probably just inline this into `validatePodMetadataAndSpec`:

```
	if securityContext != nil {
		allErrs = append(allErrs, validateHostNetwork(securityContext.HostNetwork, spec.Containers, specPath.Child("containers"))...)
	}
```

edit: after reviewing above, we still need to do *some* validation on hostnetwork ports in workload specs, so we may not need the separate function _or_ the inline if we plumb host port validation options down to a shared validation method. See comment above.

going into slow-motion skew visualization mode...

1. This relaxes validation in 1.28 by not checking hostPort values at all when validating workloads, which goes too far.
    * We want to tolerate a hostPort of 0 to allow late-defaulting, but we _don't_ want to tolerate `HostPort != 0 && HostPort != ContainerPort`.
    * I think we still need to validate host ports in all cases, but tolerate zero values when validating workloads and forbid them when validating pods. That can be plumbed as a `RequireHostPortMatchContainerPort bool` in `PodValidationOptions`, and only set to true in ValidatePodCreate/ValidatePodUpdate.

2. Normally we have to roll out validation changes over two releases, _but_ this is tolerating a zero value a 1.27 API server will default, so I _think_ we're ok
    * If a 1.28 server persists a workload with hostPort=0, a 1.27 server will default `hostPort` to match `containerPort` when reading from etcd, passing validation and avoiding a generation bump.
    * If a client reads a workload with hostPort=0 from a 1.28 server, and writes to a 1.27 server, the 1.27 server will default `hostPort` to match `containerPort` when reading from etcd, passing validation and avoiding a generation bump.

Drop AvailableResources from controller context

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Drops AvailableResources from controller context. Any controller using this information to decide whether to run or not at startup is not resilient to changes in available resources during runtime or flakes in discovery resolution at controller startup.

The only default-enabled uses in-tree were of GA enabled-by-default APIs.

The comment by the GetAvailableResources function (`TODO: In general, any controller checking this needs to be dynamic`) was explicitly unsupported by the fixed-at-startup population in the controller context.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
The `horizontalpodautoscaling` and `clusterrole-aggregation` controllers now assume the `autoscaling/v1` and `rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1` APIs are available. If you disable those APIs and do not want to run those controllers, exclude them by passing `--controllers=-horizontalpodautoscaling` or `--controllers=-clusterrole-aggregation` to `kube-controller-manager`.
```

Fix duplicate GC event handlers getting added if discovery flutters
Preserve resourcequota informers for groups with discovery resolution errors only

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

The GC controller regularly (every 30 seconds) rediscovers available API types. Discovery can partially fail, resulting in a partial view of resource types available.

The GC controller then registers new event handlers for newly visible resource types, and forgets about event handlers for no-longer-visible resource types. This forgetting is problematic in multiple ways:
* we don't unregister the event handlers (being fixed in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/114067, but there are open questions about what to do on cleanup)
* we overaggressively forget about event handlers which had already synced just because their API group/version blipped during discovery, and then recreate/register duplicates for the same resources when discovery recovers.

This PR keeps existing already-synced event handlers in place as long as discovery is failing to resolve their group/version.

In the resource quota controller, we already preserved previously seen resources in discovery error cases for similar reasons, this PR _narrows_ that retention to just group/versions with discovery resolution errors and adds test coverage for it.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
Fixes an issue with the garbagecollection controller registering duplicate event handlers if discovery requests fail.
```

Drop zeitgeist from tools go.mod

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Drops zeitgeist from tools go.mod since it pulls in large numbers of transitive dependencies (https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/zeitgeist/issues/543) and we don't need the go.mod reference for reproducible version installation.

The downside of this change is that `cd hack/tools && go mod download` is no longer sufficient to ensure code needed to build tools is pulled to the local machine, but running verify scripts once locally would cache the modules.

Alternative to https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/118050

```release-note
NONE
```

Relaxing validation has to be done over two releases. Otherwise, we allow data to be persisted that will be considered invalid by the previous release of the API server (which could still be running in a multi-server cluster during rolling upgrade).

I would suggest doing the following:

1. in `pkg/api/pod/util.go`, add an `AllowUnprivilegedBidirectionalMountPropagation bool` field to `PodValidationOptions`
2. make sure that options struct is passed through all validation functions down to this location
3. in this function, only allow the relaxed validation if the `AllowUnprivilegedBidirectionalMountPropagation` field is true
4. in `GetValidationOptionsFromPodSpecAndMeta`, compute the validation options based on the new and old object
    * in this release
      * on create (new object is non-nil, old object is nil), set the `AllowUnprivilegedBidirectionalMountPropagation` field to `false`
      * on update, set the `AllowUnprivilegedBidirectionalMountPropagation` field to `true` *only* if the old object already has bidirectional mount propagation in an unprivileged container
    * in the next release, set the `AllowUnprivilegedBidirectionalMountPropagation` field to `true` unconditionally

comment_text: Update google.golang.org/genproto

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Updates to a version of google.golang.org/genproto with generic packages carved into submodules (https://github.com/googleapis/go-genproto/pull/1012)

These still include a backreference to the root google.golang.org/genproto module, but only for a period of time while consumers are transitioning to the new isolated modules.

xref #113366 

See https://github.com/googleapis/go-genproto/issues/1015 for details on the timeline for completing the submodule isolation.

This increases the total number of dependencies in our graph because it updates to a `cloud.google.com/go` that carved out submodules for every API:
```diff
-Direct Dependencies: 205 
-Transitive Dependencies: 228 
-Total Dependencies: 277 
-Max Depth Of Dependencies: 23 
+Direct Dependencies: 209 
+Transitive Dependencies: 341 
+Total Dependencies: 390 
+Max Depth Of Dependencies: 17
```

That's unfortunate in the short-term, but does not actually increase the number of vendored/built dependencies significantly, and unblocks [several other dependency updates](https://github.com/orgs/kubernetes/projects/111/views/6?filterQuery=is%3Apr+status%3A%22Blocked+on+cloud.google.com%22). Once genproto rpc/api/bytestream packages drop the back-reference to the root genproto library on 2024-03-01, we can drop those dependencies from our graph.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
NONE
```

/hold

standard questions when we want to tighten validation:
* does it prevent a no-op update of existing persisted objects that would fail this new validation? (would block storage migration tasks)
* does it prevent deletion of existing persisted objects with finalizers that would fail this new validation? (update is required to remove finalizers to allow deletion)
* does it prevent controllers from updating status of existing persisted objects that would fail this new validation?

we could consider ratcheting validation:
* apply this tightened validation on create
* apply this tightened validation on update where the old object passed this validation (don't let a valid object become invalid)
* skip this tightened validation on update where the old object fails this validation

c.f. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/59984

comment_text:
If we do tighten validation using ratcheting, it would look like:

1. in `pkg/apis/.../validation/validation.go`, add an `Allow... bool` field to `...ValidationOptions`
2. make sure that options struct is passed through all validation functions down to this location
3. in this function, only allow the relaxed validation if the `Allow...` field is true
4. in `GetValidationOptionsFrom...`, compute the validation options based on the new and old object
    * in this release
      * on create (new object is non-nil, old object is nil), set the `Allow...` field to `false`
      * on update, set the `Allow...` field to `true` *only* if the old object already has ...
    * in the next release, set the `Allow...` field to `true` unconditionally

+func createPDBUsingRemovedAPI(ctx context.Context, etcdClient *clientv3.Client, etcdStoragePrefix, nsName string, betaPDB *v1beta1.PodDisruptionBudget) error {
+	betaPDB.APIVersion = v1beta1.SchemeGroupVersion.Group + "/" + v1beta1.SchemeGroupVersion.Version
+	betaPDB.Kind = "PodDisruptionBudget"
+	betaPDB.Namespace = nsName
+	betaPDB.Generation = 1
+	ctx = genericapirequest.WithNamespace(ctx, nsName)
+	key := path.Join("/", etcdStoragePrefix, "poddisruptionbudgets", nsName, betaPDB.Name)
+	val, _ := json.Marshal(betaPDB)
+	_, err := etcdClient.Put(ctx, key, string(val))

standard questions when we want to tighten validation:
* does it prevent a no-op update of existing persisted objects that would fail this new validation? (would block storage migration tasks)
* does it prevent deletion of existing persisted objects with finalizers that would fail this new validation? (update is required to remove finalizers to allow deletion)
* does it prevent controllers from updating status of existing persisted objects that would fail this new validation?

we could consider ratcheting validation:
* apply this tightened validation on create
* apply this tightened validation on update where the old object passed this validation (don't let a valid object become invalid)
* skip this tightened validation on update where the old object fails this validation

+package proxy

+	"io"
+	"net/http"
+	"time"
+
+	apierrors "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/errors"
+	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/httpstream/wsstream"
+	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/remotecommand"
+	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime"
+	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
+)
+
+const defaultIdleConnectionTimeout = 4 * time.Hour
+
+// Options contains details about which streams are required for
+// remote command execution.
+type options struct {
+	stdin  bool
+	stdout bool
+	stderr bool
+	tty    bool
+}
+
+// NewOptions creates a new Options from the Request.
+func streamOptionsFromRequest(req *http.Request) (*options, error) {
+	query := req.URL.Query()
+	tty := query.Get("tty") == "true"
+	stdin := query.Get("stdin") == "true"
+	stdout := query.Get("stdout") == "true"
+	stderr := query.Get("stderr") == "true"
+	if tty && stderr {
+		// TODO: make this an error before we reach this method
+		klog.Infoln("Access to exec with tty and stderr is not supported, bypassing stderr")
+		stderr = false
+	}
+
+	if !stdin && !stdout && !stderr {
+		return nil, fmt.Errorf("you must specify at least 1 of stdin, stdout, stderr")
+	}
+
+	return &options{
+		stdin:  stdin,
+		stdout: stdout,
+		stderr: stderr,
+		tty:    tty,
+	}, nil
+}
+
+// conns contains the connection and streams used when
+// forwarding an attach or execute session into a container.
+type conns struct {
+	conn         io.Closer
+	stdinStream  io.ReadCloser
+	stdoutStream io.WriteCloser
+	stderrStream io.WriteCloser
+	writeStatus  func(status *apierrors.StatusError) error
+	resizeStream io.ReadCloser
+	resizeChan   chan remotecommand.TerminalSize
+	tty          bool
+}
+
+// Create WebSocket server streams to respond to a WebSocket client. Creates the streams passed
+// in the stream options.
+func webSocketServerStreams(req *http.Request, w http.ResponseWriter, opts *options) (*conns, error) {
+	ctx, err := createWebSocketStreams(req, w, opts)
+	if err != nil {
+		return nil, err
+	}
+
+	if ctx.resizeStream != nil {
+		ctx.resizeChan = make(chan remotecommand.TerminalSize)
+		go handleResizeEvents(req.Context(), ctx.resizeStream, ctx.resizeChan)
+	}
+
+	return ctx, nil
+}
+
+// Read terminal resize events off of passed stream and queue into passed channel.
+func handleResizeEvents(ctx context.Context, stream io.Reader, channel chan<- remotecommand.TerminalSize) {
+	defer runtime.HandleCrash()
+	defer close(channel)
+
+	decoder := json.NewDecoder(stream)
+	for {
+		size := remotecommand.TerminalSize{}
+		if err := decoder.Decode(&size); err != nil {
+			break
+		}
+
+		select {
+		case channel <- size:
+		case <-ctx.Done():
+			// To avoid leaking this routine, exit if the http request finishes. This path
+			// would generally be hit if starting the process fails and nothing is started to
+			// ingest these resize events.
+			return
+		}
+	}
+}
+
+// createChannels returns the standard channel types for a shell connection (STDIN 0, STDOUT 1, STDERR 2)
+// along with the approximate duplex value. It also creates the error (3) and resize (4) channels.
+func createChannels(opts *options) []wsstream.ChannelType {
+	// open the requested channels, and always open the error channel
+	channels := make([]wsstream.ChannelType, 5)
+	channels[remotecommand.StreamStdIn] = readChannel(opts.stdin)
+	channels[remotecommand.StreamStdOut] = writeChannel(opts.stdout)
+	channels[remotecommand.StreamStdErr] = writeChannel(opts.stderr)
+	channels[remotecommand.StreamErr] = wsstream.WriteChannel
+	channels[remotecommand.StreamResize] = wsstream.ReadChannel
+	return channels
+}
+
+// readChannel returns wsstream.ReadChannel if real is true, or wsstream.IgnoreChannel.
+func readChannel(real bool) wsstream.ChannelType {
+	if real {
+		return wsstream.ReadChannel
+	}
+	return wsstream.IgnoreChannel

This comment applies generally, but I'm *really* unsure about what PSS version to attach this change to.

In the future, once host namespace graduates to GA, and the oldest supported kubelets support it, we will add a version-bound revision to PSS that unconditionally relaxes validation for user-namespaced pods.

But for this revision, because it is feature-gated, it is _not_ the case that all 1.28 servers will agree on pods that should be admitted with this relaxed validation. Some 1.28 servers would reject pods, others would accept them. We're already relying on admins setting the feature gate to know and verify all the nodes in the cluster support the user namespace feature. If we're already going to be inconsistent across servers with this behavior, I'm wondering if we should just add the feature-gated conditional exception to the existing methods like allowPrivilegeEscalation_1_25. That would avoid duplicating all the test fixtures for 1.28 when 1.28 doesn't really represent a reliable change to PSS.

Add docs indicating this should *only* be enabled if all nodes in the cluster support the user namespace feature and have it enabled, and that the feature gate will not graduate or be enabled by default.

+type fallbackExecutor struct {
+	primary   Executor
+	secondary Executor
+}
+
+// NewFallbackExecutor creates an Executor that first attempts to use the
+// WebSocketExecutor, falling back to the legacy SPDYExecutor if the initial
+// websocket "StreamWithContext" call fails.
+func NewFallbackExecutor(config *restclient.Config, method string, url *url.URL) (Executor, error) {
+	primary, err := NewWebSocketExecutor(config, method, url.String())
+	if err != nil {
+		return nil, err
+	}
+	secondary, err := NewSPDYExecutor(config, method, url)
+	if err != nil {
+		return nil, err
+	}
+	return &fallbackExecutor{
+		primary:   primary,
+		secondary: secondary,
+	}, nil
+}
+
+// Stream is deprecated. Please use "StreamWithContext".
+func (f *fallbackExecutor) Stream(options StreamOptions) error {
+	return f.StreamWithContext(context.Background(), options)
+}
+
+// StreamWithContext initially attempts to call "StreamWithContext" using the
+// primary executor, falling back to calling the secondary executor if the
+// initial primary call to upgrade to a websocket connection fails.
+func (f *fallbackExecutor) StreamWithContext(ctx context.Context, options StreamOptions) error {
+	err := f.primary.StreamWithContext(ctx, options)
+	if _, isErr := err.(*websocket.UpgradeFailureError); isErr {
+		return f.secondary.StreamWithContext(ctx, options)

+	// policyDependencies is the (policy name) -> (required CRD GroupResource) mapping.

+// convertCRDValidationToSchema converts the openAPIV3Schema to a kube-openapi schema.
+// In case of nil validation or missing OpenAPIV3Schema, this function returns ErrSchemaNotFound.
+// The CustomResourceValidation object must be valid. Anything from the informer or client should have already passed
+// validation.
+// This function uses a JSON marshal-unmarshal conversion instead of Structural because
+//   - OpenAPIV3Schema is already validated and thus always a valid OpenAPI schema;
+//   - Structural involves private api-extensions API types. We have import-boss to prevent anything outside
+//     apiextensions-apiserver package to use them.
+func convertCRDValidationToSchema(validation *apiextensionsv1.CustomResourceValidation) (*spec.Schema, error) {
+	if validation == nil || validation.OpenAPIV3Schema == nil {
+		return nil, resolver.ErrSchemaNotFound
+	}
+	b, err := json.Marshal(validation.OpenAPIV3Schema)
+	if err != nil {
+		return nil, err
+	}
+	s := new(spec.Schema)
+	err = json.Unmarshal(b, s)
+	if err != nil {
+		return nil, err
+	}
+	return s, nil
+}
+
+func newCRDTracker(controller *Controller) *crdTracker {
+	return &crdTracker{
+		affectedPolicies:   make(map[schema.GroupResource]sets.Set[string]),
+		policyDependencies: make(map[string]sets.Set[schema.GroupResource]),
+		parent:             controller,
+	}

> I am going to say I am convinced that your original approach (explict int32 API, callers need to bounds-check) is OK except for the serialization discussion, which I will recap here.

~~Is the current state of the PR the approach you're wanting to go with?~~ (edit: it doesn't appear so. I think I agree with the `explict int32 API, callers need to bounds-check` summary) Keeping the unsized int signature marked as Deprecated or removing it entirely seems better than making it silently truncate. The FromInt32 signature seems better to me. Callers can use int32 themselves or cast and bounds-check.

Serialization-wise, int-or-string should already be safe since it encodes as int32 already, right?

> Lastly, should we encourage/require net new numeric fields to be int64 and to do range checking (e.g. 1M replicas should be enough for anyone) ?

Since int64 values outside of +/- `2^53-1` don't round-trip reliably in json/yaml, I'd keep int32 as the default recommendation for integer fields. If you really need int64, you have to bounds-check values to be within the range that can actually round-trip in all our supported serialization formats.

Avoid returning nil responseKind in v1beta1 aggregated discovery

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/kind regression

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Applies fixup to returned aggregated discovery v1beta1 to avoid sending nil responseKind fields, which v0.26.0-v0.26.3 clients cannot handle.

#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
xref https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/118361 https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/116603 https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/119840

Even though the nil panic in client-go was fixed in 0.26.4+, there is widespread usage of impacted versions (https://grep.app/search?q=k8s.io/client-go%20v0%5C.26%5C.%5B0123%5D&regexp=true&filter[path.pattern][0]=go.mod)

Mitigating this server-side for v1beta1 seems prudent to avoid breaking clients when 1.27+ servers enable aggregated discovery by default.

cc @apelisse @seans3 @dims

Fixes a regression in 1.27 default server configurations: Aggregated discovery now returns `responseKind: {}` for resources which are missing group/version/kind information, to ensure compatibility with v0.26.0-v0.26.3 clients.

2. If the webhook taking too long was *why* the context timed out, shouldn't we still record that in metrics somehow? This looks like we lose all insights into webhook latency if the overall request times out.

comment_text:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/118384 updated evanphx/json-patch from 4.12.0 to 5.6.0, but `v5.6.0+incompatible` is *older* than `v4.12.0+incompatible` (both were tagged on master, and the `+incompatible` bit means this is referencing the content in the root of the repo and not the content inside the `v5` subdirectory) ... and dropped the following fix:

https://github.com/evanphx/json-patch/compare/v5.6.0...v4.12.0

This probably regresses https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/105896

It's not clear why that PR bumped to 5.6.0, or if there's a newer commit we can use on master that will result in a pseudoversion off the 5.6.0 stream.

(We can't actually switch to /v5 of the module without also picking up v5 content that caused us compatibility problems in the past (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/91622#issuecomment-637087847))

/kind regression
/priority critical-urgent
/sig api-machinery

+import (

+	"io"
+	"os"
+
+	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime"
+	api "k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/authorization/config"
+	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/authorization/config/scheme"
+	apiv1 "k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/authorization/config/v1alpha1"
+)
+
+func LoadFromFile(file string) (*api.AuthorizationConfiguration, error) {
+	if len(file) == 0 {
+		// no file specified, use default config
+		return LoadFromData(nil)
+	}
+
+	// read from file
+	data, err := os.ReadFile(file)
+	if err != nil {
+		return nil, err
+	}
+	return LoadFromData(data)
+}
+
+func LoadFromReader(reader io.Reader) (*api.AuthorizationConfiguration, error) {
+	if reader == nil {
+		// no reader specified, use default config
+		return LoadFromData(nil)
+	}
+
+	data, err := io.ReadAll(reader)
+	if err != nil {
+		return nil, err
+	}
+	return LoadFromData(data)
+}
+
+func LoadFromData(data []byte) (*api.AuthorizationConfiguration, error) {
+	if len(data) == 0 {
+		// no config provided, return default
+		externalConfig := &apiv1.AuthorizationConfiguration{}
+		scheme.Scheme.Default(externalConfig)
+		internalConfig := &api.AuthorizationConfiguration{}
+		if err := scheme.Scheme.Convert(externalConfig, internalConfig, nil); err != nil {
+			return nil, err
+		}
+		return internalConfig, nil
+	}

+// ValidateAuthorizationConfiguration validates a given AuthorizationConfiguration.
+func ValidateAuthorizationConfiguration(fldPath *field.Path, c *authorizationapi.AuthorizationConfiguration, knownTypes sets.String, repeatableTypes sets.String) field.ErrorList {
+	allErrs := field.ErrorList{}
+
+	seenAuthorizerTypes := sets.NewString()
+	seenWebhookNames := sets.NewString()
+	for i, a := range c.Authorizers {
+		fldPath := fldPath.Child("authorizers").Index(i)
+		aType := string(a.Type)
+		if aType == "" {
+			allErrs = append(allErrs, field.Required(fldPath.Child("type"), ""))
+			continue
+		}
+		if !knownTypes.Has(aType) {
+			allErrs = append(allErrs, field.NotSupported(fldPath.Child("type"), aType, knownTypes.List()))
+			continue
+		}
+		if seenAuthorizerTypes.Has(aType) && !repeatableTypes.Has(aType) {
+			allErrs = append(allErrs, field.Duplicate(fldPath.Child("type"), aType))
+			continue
+		}
+		seenAuthorizerTypes.Insert(aType)
+
+		switch a.Type {
+		case authorizationapi.TypeWebhook:
+			if a.Webhook == nil {
+				allErrs = append(allErrs, field.Required(fldPath.Child("webhook"), "required when type=Webhook"))
+				continue
+			}
+			allErrs = append(allErrs, ValidateWebhookConfiguration(fldPath, a.Webhook, seenWebhookNames)...)
+		default:
+			if a.Webhook != nil {
+				allErrs = append(allErrs, field.Invalid(fldPath.Child("webhook"), "non-null", "may only be specified when type=Webhook"))
+			}
+		}
+	}
+
+	return allErrs
+}

+	} else if seenNames.Has(c.Name) {

+	} else if errs := utilvalidation.IsDNS1035Label(c.Name); len(errs) != 0 {

+	Authorizers []AuthorizerConfiguration `json:"authorizers"`
+}
+
+const (
+	TypeWebhook                                      AuthorizerType = "Webhook"
+	FailurePolicyNoOpinion                           string         = "NoOpinion"
+	FailurePolicyDeny                                string         = "Deny"
+	AuthorizationWebhookConnectionInfoTypeKubeConfig string         = "KubeConfigFile"
+	AuthorizationWebhookConnectionInfoTypeInCluster  string         = "InClusterConfig"
+)
+
+type AuthorizerType string
+
+type AuthorizerConfiguration struct {
+	Type string `json:"type"`
+
+	Webhook *WebhookConfiguration `json:"webhook,omitempty"`
+}
+
+type WebhookConfiguration struct {
+	// Name used to describe the webhook
+	// This is explicitly used in monitoring machinery for metrics
+	// Note:
+	//   - Validation for this field is similar to how K8s labels are validated today.
+	// Required, with no default
+	Name string `json:"name"`
+	// The duration to cache 'authorized' responses from the webhook
+	// authorizer.
+	// Same as setting `--authorization-webhook-cache-authorized-ttl` flag
+	// Default: 5m0s
+	AuthorizedTTL metav1.Duration `json:"authorizedTTL"`
+	// The duration to cache 'unauthorized' responses from the webhook
+	// authorizer.
+	// Same as setting `--authorization-webhook-cache-unauthorized-ttl` flag
+	// Default: 30s
+	UnauthorizedTTL metav1.Duration `json:"unauthorizedTTL"`
+	// Timeout for the webhook request
+	// Default: 30s
+	Timeout metav1.Duration `json:"timeout"`
+	// The API version of the authorization.k8s.io SubjectAccessReview to
+	// send to and expect from the webhook.
+	// Same as setting `--authorization-webhook-version` flag
+	// Valid values: v1beta1, v1
+	// Required, no default value
+	SubjectAccessReviewVersion string `json:"subjectAccessReviewVersion"`
+	// Controls the authorization decision when a webhook request fails to
+	// complete or returns a malformed response or errors evaluating
+	// matchConditions.
+	// Valid values:
+	//   - NoOpinion: continue to subsequent authorizers to see if one of
+	//     them allows the request
+	//   - Deny: reject the request without consulting subsequent authorizers
+	// Required, with no default.
+	FailurePolicy string `json:"failurePolicy"`
+
+	ConnectionInfo WebhookConnectionInfo `json:"connectionInfo"`
+
+	// matchConditions is a list of conditions that must be met for a request to be sent to this
+	// webhook. An empty list of matchConditions matches all requests.
+	// There are a maximum of 64 match conditions allowed.
+	//
+	// The exact matching logic is (in order):
+	//   1. If at least one matchCondition evaluates to FALSE, then the webhook is skipped.
+	//   2. If ALL matchConditions evaluate to TRUE, then the webhook is called.
+	//   3. If at least one matchCondition evaluates to an error (but none are FALSE):
+	//      - If failurePolicy=Deny, then the webhook rejects the request
+	//      - If failurePolicy=NoOpinion, then the error is ignored and the webhook is skipped
+	MatchConditions []WebhookMatchCondition `json:"matchConditions"`
+}
+
+type WebhookConnectionInfo struct {
+	// Controls how the webhook should communicate with the server.
+	// Valid values:
+	// - KubeConfig: use the file specified in kubeConfigFile to locate the
+	//   server.
+	// - InClusterConfig: use the in-cluster configuration to call the
+	//   SubjectAccessReview API hosted by kube-apiserver. This mode is not
+	//   allowed for kube-apiserver.
+	Type string `json:"type"`
+
+	// Path to KubeConfigFile for connection info
+	// Required, if connectionInfo.Type is KubeConfig
+	KubeConfigFile *string `json:"kubeConfigFile"`
+}
+
+type WebhookMatchCondition struct {
+	// expression represents the expression which will be evaluated by CEL. Must evaluate to bool.
+	// CEL expressions have access to the contents of the SubjectAccessReview in v1 version.
+	// If version specified by subjectAccessReviewVersion in the request variable is v1beta1,
+	// the contents would be converted to the v1 version before evaluating the CEL expression.

(This came up in review of the https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/4141 KEP)

We need to make sure there's a test for audit policy rules using original (non-impersonated) user for deciding whether to log the audit event or not.

The authentication filter → audit filter → impersonation filter ordering in DefaultBuildHandlerChain should make this work properly but we definitely want to test it.

We specifically want to make sure an audit policy that filters out system users (for volume reasons) will still log a request from a non-system user impersonating one of those system users.

/sig auth
/cc @enj
/help

comment_text:
Dropping runAsNonRoot and runAsUser restrictions for user namespace pods makes sense, those changes lgtm.

I spent some time talking through this change with @mtaufen and @vinayakankugoyal, and I think disallowing privilege escalation and requiring dropping capabilities still provides significant protections, even with user namespaces. Allowing arbitrary capabilities exposes many more syscalls and kernel code paths, and I don't think typical vulnerabilities (buffer overflows, etc) are mitigated by a uid mapping. @vinayakankugoyal can provide more details. Before we relax privilege escalation and arbitrary capabilities for user namespace pods, we need to be confident we're not ending up in a less secure state.

If we get the feature gate plumbing straightened out (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/118760/files#r1373276402) and scope this down to the runAsUser / mustRunAsNonRoot checks, that works for now.
see comment at https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/118760/files#r1373287637

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind feature

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Implements reloading of the authorization configuration file. File reloads when an observed file event occurs or a 1 minute poll is encountered.

Best reviewed commit-by-commit, the validation / construction is refactored to split out the rbac / node / abac construction, and move the authorization construction to the reload functions.

Fixes #119102

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
kube-apiserver now reloads the `--authorization-config` file when it changes. Reloads increment the `apiserver_authorization_config_controller_automatic_reload_last_timestamp_seconds` timestamp metric, with `status="success"` for successful reloads and `status="failed"` for failed reloads. Failed reloads keep using the previously loaded authorization configuration.
```

Add stub methods for kube-proxy on non-linux/windows

Reverts https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/121722, re-adds stub methods to kube-proxy after https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/122373 so it type-checks and builds on platforms other than linux / windows again.

This has the following benefits:
* makes a simple `make` invocation work again (currently, running `make` explodes on darwin)
* allows using `kube-proxy --help` and `kube-proxy --write-config-to` on other platforms where *running* a proxy isn't needed
* improves visibility to kube-proxy use of methods when refactoring / building locally
* improves visibility to invalid use of build-tags within kube-proxy packages (like [OS-independent files relying on types declared in OS-dependent files](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/122878#discussion_r1459280615))

comment_text:
actually, thinking through this more, just moving the warning recorder doesn't fix the panic

the warning recorder does things like this:

```
r.writer.Header().Add("Warning", header)
```

`baseTimeoutWriter#Header()` is threadsafe, but once it returns the http.Header object, the call to Add() is *not* threadsafe. That means in a timeout case, baseTimeoutWriter#Write → copyHeaders() can still be trying to read from tw.handlerHeaders at the same time the warning recorder is calling Add() and mutating the headers.

I don't think this PR actually makes the warning header additions threadsafe. #123034 wraps the Add/Set/Del calls to make *those* threadsafe.
code_context:
@@ -1011,6 +1011,10 @@ func DefaultBuildHandlerChain(apiHandler http.Handler, c *Config) http.Handler {
 
 	handler = genericfilters.WithCORS(handler, c.CorsAllowedOriginList, nil, nil, nil, "true")
 
+	// WithWarningRecorder must be wrapped by the timeout handler
+	// to make the addition of warning headers threadsafe
+	handler = genericapifilters.WithWarningRecorder(handler)

comment_text:
oh, in the timeout case, baseTimeoutWriter#Write never makes it to the copyHeaders call and we just discard anything the warning recorded added to tw.handlerHeaders

this works
code_context:
@@ -1011,6 +1011,10 @@ func DefaultBuildHandlerChain(apiHandler http.Handler, c *Config) http.Handler {
 
 	handler = genericfilters.WithCORS(handler, c.CorsAllowedOriginList, nil, nil, nil, "true")
 
+	// WithWarningRecorder must be wrapped by the timeout handler
+	// to make the addition of warning headers threadsafe
+	handler = genericapifilters.WithWarningRecorder(handler)

```release-note
NONE
```

I think a release note is sufficient. People who happen to be using this alpha field in their go structs (who I expect will be vanishingly small) will know they need to take action when they update and their code needs a change before it will compile.

I think action required should be reserved for changes which will surprise or break people if they don't read the release notes, or which they can prepare their cluster or workloads for in advance. This is not such a change.

> it should be added to breaking changes of any kind that require user action

If we expand that section of the release notes to include every signature change of every go function in a staging library, that section of the release notes will be so noisy it will be useless. I think it should be reserved for things which require configuration or API changes, would cause problems if they were missed, and *could* be missed in a cluster upgrade. This is not a change like that.

Upgrading a cluster to the release that included this change does not require people to update their client-go libraries, so I really don't think action is required here.

> while a go build will catch the problem of the go struct that did not change for 7 years

~to be fair, this was a brand new alpha field, not one that had existed for years, so I sincerely doubt *anyone* was using it in a way that would require action on a cluster upgrade~

edit: my mistake, I mixed this up with a different fixup to a recent alpha field. This field was not alpha.

Guard empty status.acceptedNames

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

This PR makes the requirement to have non-empty status.acceptedNames before serving explicit instead of implicit.

Currently, serving code only checks for a NamesAccepted condition, which is set by the names controller at the same time status.acceptedNames are populated, but those fields can be emptied by a mutating webhook or later cleared by a poorly behaved status writer, and the serving code does not ensure it actually has non-empty plural / singular / kind / listKind values.

This makes that exceptional case error when serving instead of constructing storage with invalid configuration.

#### What type of PR is this?

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Fixes the kubectl proxy setup in the e2e test to make sure stdout and stderr are drained properly.

Normally, this doesn't matter since there's little to no output, but leaving the pipes blocked is not correct and can make the test fail if the proxy tries to output any stderr.

This happened during development of https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/123413 when we added lots of debug output to upgradeaware proxy (which kubectl proxy uses), and failed these unrelated e2e tests.

Use the websocket protocol header, verify selected protocol

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Found while working on https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/123413

1. This switches to use the standard websocket header to plumb the subprotocols between the Negotiate method and the websocket RoundTripper (this was all internal plumbing, there's no change on the wire due to this)
2. This verifies the subprotocol returned from the server is one of the requested ones and returns an upgrade error if not
3. This only returns an upgrade error if the error was a bad handshake. Normal network / timeout / TLS errors return an error that will not match IsUpgradeError and avoid triggering a fallback to spdy.

```release-note
NONE
```

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Testing with update of gengo submodules to pick up https://github.com/googleapis/go-genproto/pull/1087 as part of https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/113366

We still have one transitive dependency on genproto, which is `google.golang.org/genproto/protobuf/field_mask`, via grpc-gateway v1, which is referenced by etcd. This is dropped in grpc-gateway v2 (https://github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/pull/3317) and etcd 3.6 updates to grpc-gateway v2 (https://github.com/etcd-io/etcd/pull/16595).

This isn't intended to merge quite yet, just testing with the partial bumps we have so far

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Because go transitively propagates go.mod patch requirements, sticking on the .0 has two negative implications:
1. if any of our dependencies bumps to the latest patch, go will refuse to build us
2. if any of our dependants currently has a go version like go1.22, go will bump them to our go patch version (go1.22.0)

Since we update to latest patch versions ~immediately, keeping our go.mod in sync with .go-version will avoid both of these problems.

```release-note
NONE
```

/area code-organization
/cc @dims @howardjohn

Just got here, need to think about the skew implications.

There was just a lengthy discussion about what skew between kube-apiserver and aggregated servers are allowed / recommended / supported (https://github.com/kubernetes/website/pull/46109, https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/124533, https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/124655, api-machinery meeting https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0TXm-DGcK1k&t=31m). We don't really dictate whether aggregated API servers' use of k8s.io/apiserver library can / should be newer than kube-apiserver today. cc @deads2k as well who might have thoughts.

I agree our defaults (in kube-apiserver and k8s.io/apiserver library) should not lead to aggregated API servers trusting headers _before_ kube-apiserver sets / guards / protects them.

I think the following defaults work:
* kube-apiserver 1.31+ does not default the uid CLI flag (just like the other requestheader flags)
* kube-apiserver 1.31+ (as kube-aggregator) unconditionally clears the standard uid header when proxying (just like it clears the other standard headers)
* kube-apiserver 1.31+ (as kube-aggregator) unconditionally sets the standard uid header when proxying (just like it sets the other standard headers)
* k8s.io/apiserver library v0.31.0+ wires the uid CLI flag
* k8s.io/apiserver library v0.31.0+ NewDelegatingAuthenticationOptions() does not default the uid header; this keeps the CLI flags safe against much older kube-apiservers which aren't protecting the header; in practice, most aggregated API servers pull their auth header config from the configmap published by kube-apiserver rather than the CLI flags, so if/when their kube-apiserver publishes uid header config, they'll start using it

In the future (kube-apiserver 1.32+) we can consider making kube-apiserver also include the standard username/groups/extra/uid headers the aggregator uses (which are not configurable) in the configmap it publishes containing auth config, so that aggregation works even if kube-apiserver only set non-standard requestheader flag options
code_context:
@@ -116,6 +120,9 @@ func (s *RequestHeaderAuthenticationOptions) AddFlags(fs *pflag.FlagSet) {
 	fs.StringSliceVar(&s.UsernameHeaders, "requestheader-username-headers", s.UsernameHeaders, ""+
 		"List of request headers to inspect for usernames. X-Remote-User is common.")
 
+	fs.StringSliceVar(&s.UIDHeaders, "requestheader-uid-headers", s.UIDHeaders, ""+
+		"List of request headers to inspect for UIDs. X-Remote-Uid is suggested.")

A downstream e2e test creating / attaching / mounting / writing to a GCE PD CSI volume is working on latest 1.31 releases and failing with a 1.32.0-rc.0 kubelet. Nothing else changed between the passing and failing run (same OS level, same versions of all other components including CSI driver,  etc).

Bisected to https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/127076, when kubelet started being built with go 1.23.

[Many things changed in go 1.23 around filesystem handing](https://tip.golang.org/doc/go1.23#ospkgos), and it doesn't look like kubelet volume code reacted to those.

Building the kubelet with `winsymlink=0` resolved the issue.

> On Windows, the mode bits reported by [Lstat](https://tip.golang.org/pkg/os#Lstat) and [Stat](https://tip.golang.org/pkg/os#Stat) for reparse points changed. Mount points no longer have [ModeSymlink](https://tip.golang.org/pkg/os#ModeSymlink) set, and reparse points that are not symlinks, Unix sockets, or dedup files now always have [ModeIrregular](https://tip.golang.org/pkg/os#ModeIrregular) set. This behavior is controlled by the winsymlink setting. For Go 1.23, it defaults to winsymlink=1. Previous versions default to winsymlink=0.

> On Windows, [EvalSymlinks](https://tip.golang.org/pkg/path/filepath#EvalSymlinks) no longer evaluates mount points, which was a source of many inconsistencies and bugs. This behavior is controlled by the winsymlink setting. For Go 1.23, it defaults to winsymlink=1. Previous versions default to winsymlink=0.

There are other stdlib windows fs behavior changes as well:

> On Windows, Readlink no longer tries to normalize volumes to drive letters, which was not always even possible. This behavior is controlled by the winreadlinkvolume setting. For Go 1.23, it defaults to winreadlinkvolume=1. Previous versions default to winreadlinkvolume=0.

> On Windows, EvalSymlinks no longer tries to normalize volumes to drive letters, which was not always even possible. This behavior is controlled by the winreadlinkvolume setting. For Go 1.23, it defaults to winreadlinkvolume=1. Previous versions default to winreadlinkvolume=0.

There is still code throughout kubelet like this (as well as many uses of stat / lstat), which may not be correct in go 1.23 on windows any more:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/04ac01e9b64ca7006be0f88f0937122226723028/pkg/util/filesystem/util_windows.go#L45-L91

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/2b472fe4690c83a2b343995f88050b2a3e9ff0fa/staging/src/k8s.io/mount-utils/mount.go#L258-L270

We're still trying to narrow down what code in the Kubelet needs to be adjusted and was failing with default go 1.23 behavior, but wanted to raise this now for visibility to the 1.32 release.

/cc @msau42 @BenTheElder @aojea 
/cc @kubernetes/release-team 
/milestone v1.32
Revert to go1.22 windows filesystem stdlib behavior

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Reverts to go 1.22 windows filesystem behavior until use of Stat/Lstat/EvalSymlinks adjusts to go1.23 behavior

Fixes https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/129080

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
kubelet: fixes an issue mounting CSI volumes on Windows nodes in 1.32.0 release candidates
```

/sig storage windows release
/cc @marosset @msau42

Context: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/129080

Go 1.23 changed stdlib behavior of filesystem calls Stat / Lstat / EvalSymlinks on Windows. This broke some kubelet handling of volumes on Windows, and possibly other use of those functions. For Kubernetes 1.32, the behavior was temporarily reverted via godebug switches in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/129083, but this is not a long-term solution. Libraries like [k8s.io/mount-utils](https://github.com/kubernetes/mount-utils) can be used by downstream consumers building with go 1.23 and get unintended behavior, and the godebug opt-outs will eventually become unavailable, so we need to prepare Kubernetes to not require them as soon as possible.

As soon as possible in the 1.33 development cycle, sig-storage / sig-windows / sig-node need to sweep for use of Stat / Lstat / EvalSymlinks calls that would be impacted by the go 1.23 changes, and adjust them to work properly, and drop the change in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/129083 to restore default go stdlib behavior.

A prereq to doing this safely is good test coverage (both unit test level and e2e level).
- There is currently no job running unit tests on Windows
- There are some e2e jobs exercising volumes on Windows, but the one that was broken (GCE PD) does not currently have working tests (see https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/129080#issuecomment-2517810676 and https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/33905)

/sig node storage windows
/priority important-soon
/milestone v1.33
Drop use of winreadlinkvolume godebug option

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Drops use of the `winreadlinkvolume` compatibility switch, based on feedback from Microsoft AKS team that use of volume identifiers is more consistent and reliable, and that opting back into go 1.22 `winreadlinkvolume` behavior is known to break some CSI drivers. xref https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/129080#issuecomment-2522268585

The `winsymlink` compatibility switch is still required to make kubelet work properly with go 1.23 currently. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/129084 tracks adjusting as needed and removing that switch.

In terms of testing, rc.1 was released without either of these switches. rc.2 was released with both of these switches. We (GKE) ran and passed windows CSI storage tests with both of the configurations:
* just `winsymlink=0` (as part of validating https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/129083)
* both `winsymlink=0 winreadlinkvolume=0` (in rc.2)

That gives me confidence in dropping `winreadlinkvolume=0` while still passing all the tests we know of that people have been running on the release candidates.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
kubelet: on Windows, consistently resolve filesystem links to volume identifiers instead of inconsistently normalizing to drive letters
```

/sig storage windows release
/cc @msau42 @marosset @andyzhangx 
/hold for reviews and discussion

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Fixes ways our ToUnstructured convertor does not match stdlib JSON marshaling.

Discovered while working on https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/130989 and making sure new omitzero behavior was consistent with stdlib.

We didn't actually have built-in types that this fix changes, since we define JSON tags, embed our inline fields, and don't use the field name `omitempty`, but this makes apimachinery helpers correct for other types if they do.

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

xref #124347

UID and resourceVersion preconditions fire before NotFound errors when GuaranteedUpdate is given an ignoreNotFound=true argument.

We can start by only setting that for types that allow createonupdate or server-side apply requests (which allow crupdate).

This would improve the error for this case from a difficult-to-understand precondition error to a clearer NotFound error.

A follow-up could be to turn precondition errors when the object being compared has an empty uid or resourceVersion into NotFound errors.

TODO: still needs tests

```release-note
NONE
```

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Limit structural validation of the --service-account-signing-endpoint flag to validate abstract socket names. There was no particular reason extra validation of this flag for filepaths was done... other flags like that we just attempt to use the specified path and fail if it's not found, etc ... the main intent of the validation was to make sure abstract sockets passed (starting with `@`) were valid abstract socket names.

Since relative path sockets are created by integration tests, this allows external signer integration tests to pass on all platforms (they are currently failing on darwin).

Found while testing https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/131493 locally.

Currently, we update dependency versions in an ad-hoc way. Sometimes we lag by months or years.

Frequently, when we update dependencies, we discover that they added more transitive dependencies (which we generally don't want to grow), or they added links to specific unwanted dependencies.

@dims has a script that periodically checks how far our dependencies are behind their $latest release. That publishes results at https://gist.github.com/dims/c0857b86441442b15852a4c2355db130/

In addition to how far behind we are, it would also be helpful to know:
* if newer versions of our dependencies make our dependency stats better or worse
* if newer versions of our dependencies make our unwanted-dependency status better or worse
* if newer versions of our dependencies break update-vendor in some way

A forward-looking job that periodically does something like
* `go get -u ./...`
* `hack/update-vendor.sh`
* `hack/lint-dependencies.sh > unwanted-report`
* `calculate stats > stats-report`

and surfaces *changes* in those reports could help get advance visibility to stuff coming from dependencies that was going to break us, or add new unwanted links, or increase deps.

GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/storageclasses

Add synthetic create authz check to pods/exec, pods/attach, pods/portforward

### What would you like to be added?

GetPodFromTemplate currently drops any ownerReferences specified in the pod template:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f28b4c9efbca5c5c0af716d9f2d5702667ee8a45/pkg/controller/controller_utils.go#L575-L582

This means that workload authors do not have a generic way to establish belongs-to relationships between the eventual pods and other objects.

### Why is this needed?

Controllers / schedulers can require visibility to connections from higher-level ancestor objects grouping sets of related pods.

There are implications to propagating owner references we would need to think through and derisk:
* if controllerRef is nil and all template.OwnerReferences are missing, new pods would get GCed immediately
* if controllerRef is non-nil, and template.OwnerReferences has a `isController:true`, pod creation would fail validation
* if controllerRef is non-nil and the controller was counting on generic GC to clean up pods, it would now wait until all template.OwnerReferences are gone too

Revert "protect against race between deletion and adding finalizers"

This reverts commit 1dd33c8e6de2428bb0bb50142518158764fbb942.

This reverts commit 1dd33c8e6de2428bb0bb50142518158764fbb942. After https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/129768 merged, some e2e tests started flaking with "not found" errors when attempting to delete pods https://storage.googleapis.com/k8s-triage/index.html?text=delet.*not%20found Reported in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/133976 I suspect that there were existing races with pod deletion that the unconditional delete masked with this path: ``` // Please refer to the place where we set ignoreNotFound for the reason // why we ignore the NotFound error . if storage.IsNotFound(err) && ignoreNotFound && lastExisting != nil { // The lastExisting object may not be the last state of the object // before its deletion, but it's the best approximation. out, err := e.finalizeDelete(ctx, lastExisting, true, options) return out, true, err } ``` Reverting to get CI back to healthy state, will dig into what was happening with those tests and will reintroduce making the retry-on-conflict path behave as well on a NotFound error. ```release-note NONE ``` /cc @aojea @deads2k

Reattempt of https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/129768, which was reverted in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/133979

The first merge started flaking e2e tests on "not found" errors deleting pods. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/133976

I found and addressed two gaps in the original PR in subsequent commits with tests:
* When a graceful deletion object is both updated and then immediately deleted in the store#Delete function, if something else deleted the object in between, Delete tolerates a NotFound error and returns the last updated object. If the Delete now fails on an RV precondition error, the Get we do on the reattempt should also react to a NotFound error in the same way to preserve user-facing behavior.
* When a graceful deletion object is both updated and deleted in the store#Delete function, the RV from the updated object must be used as the internal RV precondition.

```release-note
kube-apiserver: fixes a race condition which allowed delete API requests to successfully delete API objects which had a finalizer added concurrently
```

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup
/kind feature

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Completes the first phase of https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5589

1. Drops all runtime dependencies on the unmaintained gogo protobuf from kube API types 
2. Drops unused generated protobuf methods
3. Relocates the `ProtoMessage()` marker method on Kubernetes REST API types behind a `kubernetes_protomessage_one_more_release` build tag. This was a misleading marker method, since gogo-generated types are not fully compliant with what the standard protobuf library expects (it describes these as "aberrant messages" and has only best-effort support for them)

These changes are accomplished by updating our existing post-gogo-generation rewrite step to:
* relocate empty `ProtoMessage()` methods to a separate build-tagged file
* drop unused proto registrations, type assertions, and variable declarations using gogo proto types
* switch from a gogo String sorter utility function to use sort.Strings directly

This limits gogo dependencies for kube API types to code-generator generation time

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5589

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/96564

```release-note
Kubernetes API Go types removed runtime use of the github.com/gogo/protobuf library, and are no longer registered into the global gogo type registry. Kubernetes API Go types were not suitable for use with the google.golang.org/protobuf library, and no longer implement `ProtoMessage()` by default to avoid accidental incompatible use. If removal of these marker methods impacts your use, it can be re-enabled for one more release with a `kubernetes_protomessage_one_more_release` build tag, but will be removed in 1.36.
```

Include relevant dimensions in pod controller indexing

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

The pod controller uid index currently only includes the uid of the owner reference.

The namespace, kind, and name of the owner must also match for the owner reference to be considered a match.

This PR includes those dimensions in the index, unifies the key building between orphaned and non-orphaned pods, and makes all accesses go through the key builder to ensure there is no direct use of OrphanPodIndexKey.

This should be picked to release branches that introduced controller uid indexing (1.33 and 1.34) to resolve possible controller confusion in the presence of incorrect ownerReference uids.

See https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/92743 for similar issues the garbage collection controller hit when indexing only on uid.

```release-note
kube-controller-manager: Resolves potential issues handling pods with incorrect uids in their ownerReference
```

Add unit test detecting spurious statefulset rollout

Remove unused and fragile revision hash comparisons

This was broken since 666a41c2ea41d4ea19cd132c36dd7e5243572806 when the label value became non-integer encoded
The chance of one controller revision hash label being int-parsable: 7/27 ^ 8 = 0.00002041 = ~0
The chance of both being int-parsable: 0.00002041^2 = ~0

Hash comparison locks in differences in content failing EqualRevision
even when the semantic content is normalized to be equal.

/sig node

When kubelet reads pod manifests from disk, it bypasses API server defaulting / strategy initialization

It tries to replicate that by:
1. decoding with defaulting (which replicates API server defaulting): 
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a90cf500049d228f5ee4d217c4714028eb9c5f35/pkg/kubelet/config/common.go#L115-L122

2. applying its own calculated versions of generated fields like uid / generated name / etc:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a90cf500049d228f5ee4d217c4714028eb9c5f35/pkg/kubelet/config/common.go#L60-L82

However, it is missing any fields set at pod creation time by podStrategy#PrepareForCreate:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a90cf500049d228f5ee4d217c4714028eb9c5f35/pkg/registry/core/pod/strategy.go#L86-L110

This is likely to break assumptions in the kubelet that every pod it encounters has gone through those strategy methods. We should consider having the kubelet call both of those functions on static pods.

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Drops the temporarily emitted build-tagged ProtoMessage implementations on Kubernetes REST API types.

These were extracted from gogo-generated methods in 1.35 to give a temporary mitigation to consumers who relied on these types implementing ProtoMessage().

Scanning public repositories, only a [single use](https://github.com/search?q=kubernetes_protomessage_one_more_release+-language%3Amarkdown+-language%3Ajson+-language%3Ayaml+-org%3Akubernetes+-path%3Acmd%2Fgo-to-protobuf&type=code) of this mitigation was spotted, and I've [commented on that use](https://github.com/karmada-io/karmada/pull/7149/commits/cb4eb0d4ba6822e2ad4088b41480f684fa05f3be#r2817583948) to ensure the author is aware of the need to stop relying on this (misleading/incorrect) marker method.


#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5589

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
k8s.io/api: REST API types no longer implement marker `ProtoMessage()` methods, identifying them as standard v1 proto messages. Protobuf serialization of Kubernetes API types should use [k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime/serializer/protobuf](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime/serializer/protobuf). See [KEP-5589](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/5589-gogo-dependency) for more details.
```

Make quota evaluators in admission conditional on enabled resources
Make API group enablement accurate for generic API server

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

kube-apiserver and generic API server can be started with specific API groups disabled from being served.

When this is done, quota evaluators set up for admission are unused, but may start informers on the unserved resources which would never sync, and would block API server healthz.

xref https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135048#discussion_r2759312785

This PR does two things:
1. Splits the API resource configuration into generic / non-generic API groups, and makes the sample generic API server only explicitly register the API groups it can serve
2. Makes admission quota evaluators conditional on the resource it is protecting actually being served

While reviewing https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137028/files, I noticed that any addition of any group/version results in changes to the openapi v3 published for all other group versions.

This happens because we cross-register generic metav1 types like DeleteOptions and WatchEvent into all API group/versions, and then publish all registered group/versions for those types into openapiv3 anywhere they are listed.

Consider limiting the published group/versions for things like DeleteOptions and WatchEvent to the meta.k8s.io/v1 and local group/version.

Some types like GetOptions, ListOptions, PatchOptions are only encoded to parameter query strings

Because these types are registered into runtime.Scheme, they are currently tested as serialized to json, yaml, and proto (but *not* query parameters) in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/tree/master/staging/src/k8s.io/api/testdata

That produced spurious proto diffs in PRs like https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136657 for ListOptions

1. Identify the types we don't serialize to proto (GetOptions, ListOptions, PatchOptions, I think) and do serialize to query parameters
2. Exclude those from proto round-tripping and serialization
3. Add tests to cover the stability of their query parameter serializations

I was hoping the following things would be caught by CI:
* inconsistent DV added to v1 without being added to other versions
* DV added to go types without being wired up to actually be called by the strategy (and/or called by all subresource strategies, and/or called correctly without switching new/old object arg order, etc)
* DV added without any corresponding test coverage
